item.title: Pope Leo, attacked again by Trump, flies to Cameroon on Africa tour
item.description:
ABOARD THE PAPAL FLIGHT, April 15 (Reuters) - Pope Leo set off for Cameroon on Wednesday, ÔÇîwhere he is expected to appeal for peace in the simmering conflict in the country's English-speaking regions on the second leg of an ambitious 10-day tour of four African countries.
Leo, who was attacked again , opens new tab overnight by U.S. President Donald Trump over ÔÇőthe pope's opposition to the Iran war , will arrive from Algeria, where he blasted violations of international ÔÇőlaw by "neocolonial" world powers.
Leo left Algiers on Wednesday morning and was expected to Ôüáland in Yaounde, Cameroon's capital, around 3:20 p.m. (1420 GMT), ahead of a meeting with President ÔÇőPaul Biya and an address to national leaders.
Cameroon is a former German colony that was divided by ÔÇőBritain and France after World War One. Over the last decade, thousands have died in violence between government forces and separatist groups in its two anglophone regions.
A separatist alliance said on Monday it would observe a three-day "safe travel ÔÇőpassage" to allow civilians and visitors to move freely during the pope's visit.
Leo, originally from Chicago, ÔÇőwas chosen to lead the 1.4 billion-member Catholic Church last May after the death of Pope Francis .
He kept a relatively ÔÇîlow Ôüáprofile in his first 10 months, but in recent weeks has become outspoken on a range of issues, and a firm critic of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Trump called the pontiff " terrible " on social media on Sunday, and doubled down on Tuesday night , despite widespread ÔÇőbacklash from U.S. Christians across ÔÇőthe political spectrum.
The Ôüápope told Reuters on Monday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, regardless of Trump's comments.
Leo, aged 70, relatively young for a pope and in ÔÇőgood health, is undertaking one of the most complicated tours arranged for ÔÇőa pontiff Ôüáin decades.
He is traversing nearly 18,000 km (11,185 miles) over 18 flights to 11 cities and towns and will also visit Angola and Equatorial Guinea.
The pope will travel on Thursday to Bamenda, Cameroon's largest English-speaking Ôüácity, holding ÔÇőMass and a "meeting for peace" in a cathedral.
The biggest event ÔÇőof Leo's tour will likely come in Cameroon on Friday, when the Vatican said some 600,000 are expected for a ÔÇőMass in the coastal city of Douala.

item.title: Hungary election winner Magyar expects to form government by mid-May
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Election winner Peter Magyar said ÔÇőon Wednesday that ÔÇîPresident Tamas Sulyok would ask him at ÔÇőthe inaugural session ÔÇőof parliament to form ÔüáHungary's next government, ÔÇőwhich could be sworn ÔÇőin by the middle of next month or ÔÇősomewhat sooner.
"The President ÔÇőhas informed me that he ÔÇîwill Ôüáask me at the inaugural session of the new parliament ÔÇőto ÔÇőbe ÔüáPrime Minister and form a government, ÔÇőas the ÔÇőleader Ôüáof the party that got the most Ôüávotes," ÔÇőMagyar said ÔÇőafter meeting Sulyok.

item.title: Hungary's election winner Magyar to meet president, calls on him to resign
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Hungary's election ÔÇőwinner Peter Magyar, ÔÇîleader of the TISZA ÔÇőparty which ÔÇőwon a landslide Ôüávictory in ÔÇőSunday's election, ÔÇőarrived for a meeting with President ÔÇőTamas Sulyok ÔÇőon Wednesday.
As Magyar ÔÇîwas Ôüágoing into the meeting, speaking to reporters ÔÇőhe ÔÇőreiterated Ôüáthat Sulyok must ÔÇőleave his ÔÇőrole Ôüáafter a TISZA Ôüágovernment ÔÇőtakes office.

item.title: China's Xi calls for strategic clarity, political security with Vietnam
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping called for ÔÇőChina and Vietnam to ÔÇîmaintain a high degree of strategic clarity and ÔÇőprioritise safeguarding political security ÔÇőduring a meeting with Ôüáhis Vietnamese counterpart on ÔÇőWednesday.
Vietnam's top leader, To ÔÇőLam, is in China until Friday in his first overseas ÔÇőtrip since being ÔÇőelected state president last week, a ÔÇîsign Ôüáof warming ties between the two Communist neighbours.
The countries have grown closer ÔÇőin ÔÇőrecent Ôüáyears, cooperating in railways, technology and ÔÇőother sectors, even ÔÇőas Ôüáthey remain at odds over maritime boundaries in Ôüáthe ÔÇőSouth China Sea.

item.title: Portugal, unlike Spain, rejects separate European army
item.description:
LISBON, April 15 (Reuters) - Portugal is against the creation of a separate European army and instead backs strengthening and modernising its armed forces within the ÔÇőU.S.-led NATO alliance, the country's defence minister said, contrasting with neighbouring ÔÇîSpain's position.
Nuno Melo said late on Tuesday that Portugal was a founding NATO member which valued the United States, describing Washington as a fundamental transatlantic partner.
"We're not in favour ÔÇőof a single European army," he told a parliamentary committee, adding that ÔÇőPortugal needed to invest in its armed forces to ensure that Ôüáthey were able to fulfil their assigned missions within NATO.
Amid doubts over ÔÇőU.S. President Donald Trump's commitment to Europe's security, Spain has urged the European Union ÔÇőto move towards creating a separate joint army as a deterrent.
NATO chief Mark Rutte has dismissed calls for a European army, arguing it would cost member states far more than the ÔÇő5% of gross domestic product that NATO countries - except Spain - have agreed to ÔÇőspend on defence and related investments by 2035. Madrid said it could meet its commitments ÔÇîby Ôüáspending 2.1% of GDP .
Melo said that Portugal increased its defence spending, under NATO criteria, to 6.12 billion euros ($7.22 billion) - or 2% of GDP - in 2025, four years ahead of the original schedule. In 2024, spending was around 4.5 billion euros, ÔÇőor 1.58% of GDP.
Spain ÔÇőalso reached its Ôüátarget of 2% of GDP last year, spending 33.5 billion euros - a 44.5% increase from 2024.
Portugal has applied for ÔÇő5.8 billion euros in lowÔÇĹcost EU loans to strengthen its ÔÇőarmed forces, ÔüáMelo said. The new equipment including frigates, armoured vehicles, satellites and drones was set to be delivered by 2030, "if all goes well", he added.
The programme, known as ÔüáSecurity ÔÇőAction for Europe (SAFE), is a 150 billion euro ÔÇőEU initiative aimed at reinforcing defence capabilities, closing critical gaps and jointly procuring equipment to counter potential ÔÇőthreats to the bloc.
($1 = 0.8477 euros)

item.title: Japan naphtha-dependent firms flag supply issues despite government assurances
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japanese companies whose output is dependent on naphtha or naphtha-based products are halting orders or slashing production, highlighting ÔÇőa disconnect between official assurances of sufficient supply and realities on the ground.
Over the past week, more than a dozen firms ÔÇîincluding Toto (5332.T) , opens new tab and Asahi Kasei (3407.T) , opens new tab have flagged disruption in delivery or hiked prices, citing concern over the procurement of materials such as adhesives made from naphtha - an oil derivative chiefly used as a solvent or diluent.
Manufacturers of thinner-related products such as Kansai Paint (4613.T) , opens new tab , which by law can only hold limited stock of the hazardous material, have also adjusted delivery arrangements and ÔÇőraised prices, clouding the outlook downstream for goods and services as varied as plastic toys and housing construction.
Just 2.7% of companies are able ÔÇőto obtain thinner as usual, showed a survey conducted last week by the Japan Painting Contractors Association.
The picture amounts Ôüáto a headache for the government, which has sought to prevent disruption by repeating its message that Japan has enough naphtha for the next four months.
The ÔÇőgovernment has said it is working to secure supplies outside the Middle East, from which Japan sourced 40% of its naphtha before the U.S. and Israel attacked ÔÇőIran on February 28. It has also set up consultation services to help businesses with procurement before they opt for changes in operations.
Even so, Toto this week suspended the acceptance of orders for modular bathroom units requiring adhesives that rely on solvents containing naphtha. Rivals Lixil (5938.T) , opens new tab , Panasonic (6752.T) , opens new tab and Cleanup (7955.T) , opens new tab have similarly flagged delivery impact.
Countries including South ÔÇőKorea , Thailand and Bangladesh have responded to disruption in the supply of Middle East oil products by calling on the public to take conservation measures, such as ÔÇőby driving less or even taking shorter showers.
Asked why Japan has not adopted a similar approach, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said she did not want to slow economic activity. She ÔÇîhas tasked Ôüáindustry minister Ryosei Akazawa with ensuring a steady supply of oil-related products.
On Tuesday, Akazawa said the "blockage" was in the middle of the supply chain, with thinner wholesalers, for example, halving supply to customers in April upon being told that shipments would be uncertain in May.
"For Prime Minister Takaichi, the economy comes first. She doesn't say anything that would be difficult for citizens," a government official involved in economic policy told Reuters, declining to be identified because of ÔÇőthe sensitivity of the matter. "This is the ÔÇőadministration's policy."
Another government official said Ôüáthe administration and industry ministry are afraid of consumer panic reminiscent of the hoarding of toilet paper and other goods during the 1970s oil crisis.
"We should really be calling on people to save energy now but the prime minister's ÔÇőoffice has put a stop to that," said the second official on condition of anonymity. "We've already said we ÔÇőhave enough supply so Ôüáif we were to ask for conservation measures, we might invite criticism, and that's the fear."
Shares of naphtha-dependent companies including Toto, Kansai Paint and Mitsubishi Chemical (4188.T) , opens new tab are underperforming Tokyo's benchmark Nikkei share price average (.N225) , opens new tab , which has now recouped most of the loss suffered since the start of Middle East conflict triggered general risk-off sentiment.
"Even Ôüáif producers ÔÇőcould ramp up output regardless of cost ... they risk being stuck with high-priced inventory if ÔÇőprices eventually drop," said Tomoichiro Kubota, senior market analyst at Matsui Securities.
"The Takaichi administration prioritises economic sentiment. While it has announced support for specific items, such an approach is like a game ÔÇőof whack-a-mole."

item.title: Nigeria orders probe and defends deadly airstrike in insurgent-held Jilli
item.description:
ABUJA, April 15 (Reuters) - Nigeria has ordered ÔÇîa full and independent investigation into a military airstrike on a market that left at least 200 people feared dead , while defending the operation that it said targeted Islamist ÔÇőmilitants based in an enclave controlled by insurgents.
Saturday's airstrike is the ÔÇőlatest to have caused heavy civilian casualties in northeast Nigeria, where Ôüábattlelines are blurred.
As Islamist militants control territories, markets and supply routes, while ÔÇőcivilians move through restricted areas for survival, rights groups have warned strikes ÔÇőon contested spaces carry a high risk of killing bystanders.
Information Minister Mohammed Idris expressed regret for civilian casualties but said the Air Force hit "confirmed terrorist targets" in Jilli village, ÔÇőa high-risk no-go zone in Gubio district that has for years served ÔÇőas a logistics hub for Boko Haram and Islamic State offshoot, ISWAP, in the ÔÇîregion.
"The ÔüáFederal Government reiterates that this was a deliberate, intelligence-led operation, not an indiscriminate attack, conducted in one of the most active insurgent corridors in the northeast," Idris said in a statement late on Tuesday.
He said the investigation ÔÇőwould look into the ÔÇőoperation's planning and Ôüáexecution.
Authorities said Jilli, known locally as the "terrorists' market", and the nearby Gazabure market were shut about five years ÔÇőago after militants took control, with intelligence showing the ÔÇőarea was Ôüáused to collect levies, buy supplies, and plan attacks, including assaults on April 9 in Ngamdu and Benisheikh that killed a general.
Militants have waged a 17-year insurgency Ôüáseeking ÔÇőto carve out an Islamic state in northeast ÔÇőNigeria, killing thousands and displacing at least 2 million people, aid groups say, despite major ÔÇőmilitary campaigns.

item.title: Tanzania investigates death of American social media influencer
item.description:
DAR ES SALAAM, April 15 (Reuters) - Police in Tanzania said they are questioning the fianc├ę of U.S. social ÔÇőmedia influencer Ashly Robinson, who died after ÔÇîan alleged suicide attempt while the couple were on vacation in Zanzibar, a tourist hub off the east ÔÇőAfrican country's mainland.
Police have confiscated the passport of ÔÇőher partner, Joseph Isaac McCann, 45, also Ôüáa U.S. citizen, they said in a statement ÔÇőissued late on Tuesday.
Robinson, 31, who is also ÔÇőknown by her social media name Ashlee Jenae, and McCann arrived in Zanzibar on April 4 and booked into a ÔÇőhotel.
They later moved to a second hotel ÔÇőwhere they "appeared to be having domestic conflicts," police said, citing ÔÇîhotel Ôüástaff. Management subsequently separated the two into different rooms.
"According to reports, she attempted to hang herself using a clothing belt tied inside a wardrobe in ÔÇőRoom 25 ÔÇőof the Ôüáhotel," the police statement said. She died in hospital on April 9 around ÔÇő9 p.m. local time while receiving ÔÇőtreatment.
"Investigations into Ôüáthe matter are ongoing, pending professional medical reports and forensic examination from doctors," police said. McCann Ôüáhas ÔÇőnot been charged. Reuters was not ÔÇőimmediately able to contact his lawyer for comment.

item.title: Serbia's president tells military to create units armed with attack drones
item.description:
BELGRADE, April 15 (Reuters) - The Serbian army should create units with attack drones, President Aleksandar Vucic said on Wednesday, as the Balkan country seeks to modernise its ÔÇőarmed forces from its obsolete Soviet-era technology.
Belgrade has already bought modern ÔÇîweapons and equipment from Europe, Israel and China and it wants to adapt its military strategy to conform with modern warfare.
Serbia is military neutral and is balancing a partnership with ÔÇőNATO and aspirations to join the European Union with its ties ÔÇőwith Russia and China.
After meeting top military commanders in Belgrade, Vucic, Ôüáthe supreme commander of the army, said he had proposed the formation of ÔÇőunits equipped with long-range attack drones and drones that hover over a strike ÔÇőarea before flying into targets.
"In addition to the increased production of drones, which I expect to explode this year ... we will significantly devote ourselves to the ... digitisation of our army," ÔÇőhe said.
"I think that in this regard we will be one of the ÔÇőarmies with the most pronounced interoperability and the greatest capabilities, not only in our region, ÔÇîbut Ôüáalso wider than that."
Serbia maintains a 22,500-strong army and in 2026 it allocated 3.3% of its GDP for defence expenditure.
Serbia's rearmament has triggered criticism from Croatia, Bosnia and Kosovo, its neighbours and wartime foes from the 1990s, who see it ÔÇőas a threat ÔÇőto regional stability. ÔüáBelgrade denies that.
Vucic's comments come after the announcement of the creation of a joint Serbian-Israeli drone manufacturing plant a day earlier.
In ÔÇőearly 2025, Serbia also bought Israel's PULS artillery systems ÔÇőand Hermes Ôüádrones manufactured by Elbit Systems (ESLT.TA) , opens new tab for $335 million and in August 2025 it also purchased long-range missiles, drones and electronic warfare equipment for around $1.6 billion.
It has bought cargo aircraft Ôüáand ÔÇőhelicopters from Airbus (AIR.PA) , opens new tab and Chinese missiles and drones.
Belgrade ÔÇőalso purchased Rafale jet fighters manufactured by France's Dassault (AM.PA) , opens new tab for 2.7 billion euros ($3.18 billion), to replace ÔÇőits ageing Soviet-made MiG-29 planes.
($1 = 0.8484 euros)

item.title: Trump adviser Sebastian Gorka seeks top counterterrorism job, WaPo reports
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's security aide ÔÇőSebastian Gorka is ÔÇîseeking to become the next head of the ÔÇőNational Counterterrorism Center, ÔÇőthe Washington Post reported Ôüáon Wednesday, nearly ÔÇőa month after the ÔÇőcenter's previous head quit due to differences over the war ÔÇőin Iran.
Joe Kent, ÔÇőwho previously headed the center, ÔÇîhad said Ôüáwhile resigning last month that Iran posed no imminent threat ÔÇőto ÔÇőthe ÔüáUnited States.
The U.S. National Counterterrorism Center ÔÇőand the White ÔÇőHouse Ôüádid not immediately respond to Reuters' requests Ôüáfor ÔÇőcomment on ÔÇőthe Washington Post report.

item.title: Swedish power plant targeted by pro-Russian group in 2025, government says
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, April 15 (Reuters) - A pro-Russian cyber group tried to disrupt operations at a Swedish thermal power plant last year, ÔÇőthe Swedish government said on Wednesday, adding that ÔÇîRussian hybrid attacks had become more frequent and serious.
Carl-Oskar Bohlin, minister for civil defence, said the group had targeted a power ÔÇőplant in western Sweden during spring of 2025, ÔÇőbut that the attack had been unsuccessful. Bohlin Ôüádid not name the plant.
"The Security Police handled ÔÇőthe case and were able to identify the actor behind ÔÇőit, who had connections to Russian intelligence and security services," Bohlin told a news conference.
"Fortunately, no serious consequences occurred due to a ÔÇőbuilt-in protection mechanism," the minister said.
The Russian embassy ÔÇőin Stockholm did not immediately reply to a request for comment but ÔÇîMoscow Ôüáregularly denies responsibility for malicious cyber activity in Europe.
A Security Police spokesperson said the agency no longer had an open investigation into the matter.
Bohlin compared the incident ÔÇőwith similar attacks in ÔÇőPoland on Ôüáenergy infrastructure, which Polish officials have blamed on Russian spy agencies .
He said Russian hybrid attacks ÔÇőhad become more frequent since Russia's invasion ÔÇőof ÔüáUkraine and recently also more dangerous.
"We have seen for some time now that pro-Russian individuals and groups that have Ôüápreviously carried ÔÇőout denial-of-service attacks are attempting ÔÇőto carry out destructive cyberattacks against businesses in Europe, also against Swedish ÔÇőtargets," he said.

item.title: Back to business
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Global stock markets are largely back near pre-war levels and Wall Street is once again flirting with record highs, as the mood music from the ÔÇőMiddle East and Washington turns positive on hopes of more peace talks .
Real or imagined, that narrative is capping crude prices, allowing investors to ÔÇîrefocus on earnings and economic fundamentals.
IÔÇÖll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column , where I dig into markets' apparent belief that the war is likely over, bar the shouting.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I discuss corporate earnings and the IMF's latest global growth forecasts.
Finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in ÔÇőyour calendar, when I'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for a timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign up ÔÇőhere , opens new tab .
Brent and WTI crude were still comfortably below $100 a barrel heading into Wednesday, trading at around $96/bbl and $92/bbl, respectively. That Ôüáfollowed comments from President Trump on Tuesday that talks with Iran could resume in days , even as the U.S. military said it had halted IranÔÇÖs maritime trade as ÔÇőpart of its naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.
This helped fuel another Wall Street rally on Tuesday. The Nasdaq jumped 2%, while the S&P 500 rose 1% ÔÇőto close just shy of its record high. Asian equities followed suit on Wednesday, with JapanÔÇÖs Nikkei up 0.9% and South KoreaÔÇÖs KOSPI surging 3%. European stocks traded flat , as did U.S. futures before the bell.
In a sign that the recovery hasn't only been on Wall Street, the MSCI all-country index, excluding U.S. stocks, hit its best levels since March 2 on Tuesday. ÔÇőElsewhere, the VIX volatility index returned to its February levels and the dollar continued to give back its safe-haven gains, hovering near its lowest point since the ÔÇőwar.
The optimism at large looks to be justified by corporate earnings, which kicked off in earnest this week with mostly bumper results from the U.S. banks. JPMorgan beat first-quarter profit expectations, helped ÔÇîby robust Ôüátrading revenue and dealmaking, while Citi reported its strongest quarterly revenue in a decade, sending its shares to their highest point since 2008. Bank of America and Morgan Stanley will report later today.
ASML, the world's largest chipmaking tool supplier, added to the upbeat tone on Wednesday as it beat earnings expectations and lifted its 2026 revenue outlook on AI-driven demand.
Although the IMF trimmed its global growth forecasts on Tuesday - and even warned the world could be drifting toward an ÔÇťadverse scenarioÔÇŁ amid the war - its reference ÔÇőforecast assumes a short-lived conflict, with ÔÇőits global growth forecast unchanged for Ôüá2027.
Meanwhile, U.S. producer prices jumped in March due to the energy shock, but the increase was almost half what economists had expected - a reassuring sign, particularly as the data covered a period after the start of the Iran conflict.
Chart of the day
As ÔÇőfinancial markets return to pre-war levels, many investors are looking at just why the March oil shock has not ÔÇőshifted the dial much Ôüáon global economic forecasts. One of many reasons is the world economy's dwindling dependency on fossil fuels, with the share of power generated by wind and solar more than trebling over the past decade.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. March import prices (8:30 a.m. EDT)
* FedÔÇÖs Michael Barr and Michelle Bowman speak
* Fed issues the Beige Book
* U.S. Ôüácorporate earnings: Bank ÔÇőof America, Morgan Stanley
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? ÔÇőSign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, ÔÇőwhich, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Hormuz pipeline workaround looks worth the cost
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Middle Eastern oil exporters need a plan B. IranÔÇÖs effective blockade of the Strait of Hormuz means Saudi Arabia, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and others might end up having to pay tolls to get their exports out to buyers around the world. Unless Tehran sets these charges at a lowball level, there's a major financial incentive for Gulf states to start building alternative pipeline routes in ÔÇőearnest.
There is little recent historical precedent for a nation to tax passage through a natural waterway. Under the 1936 Montreux Convention , opens new tab , Turkey doesnÔÇÖt charge for entry to the Black Sea, while Danish tolls for shipping to ÔÇîpass through the Oresund strait were abolished in 1857 amid international pressure. Egyptian fees for Suez Canal transit, like those levied by the Panama Canal, are different: both are artificial passages that need cash for their upkeep.
Thus far, Iran has merely said that safe passage through Hormuz hinges on coordination with its authorities, stopping short of setting a public and specific toll. Still, there is plenty of scope to charge one. Gulf oil producers stand to lose hundreds of billions of dollars per year if the strait stays shut, which suggests that Tehran has the long-term negotiating power to influence the terms ÔÇőof any re-opening.
Putting a number on the toll takings is tough. In the past month, at least one of the handful of ships that has transited the strait has paid $2 million , opens new tab ÔÇö the same figure the New York Times said , opens new tab Tehran would ÔÇőlook to levy, citing two senior Iranian officials. Trade publication LloydÔÇÖs List says , opens new tab that before the war, up to 50 tankers carrying crude, other oil products and liquefied natural gas typically passed Ôüáthrough each day, or 18,250 a year. On that basis, Tehran could theoretically pocket $37 billion annually if traffic normalises.
Other approaches yield much smaller figures, though. Another toll idea , opens new tab floating around is a $1 per barrel charge. Around 20 million daily barrels flowed through Hormuz before ÔÇőthe war, of which perhaps half could conceivably find another route based on existing infrastructure. Assume the $1 fee applied to 10 million barrels per day, then, and the annual total would be $3.7 billion per year for Tehran. That looks like the low end of ÔÇőreasonable estimates.
Even this relatively small yearly toll would stack up. Apply a 5% discount rate, which is roughly Saudi Arabia's 10-year borrowing cost, to 25 years of payments at $3.7 billion annually. The total sum is $54 billion in today's money. The question is whether that conservative number is high enough to incentivise Iran's Gulf adversaries to construct a workaround to dodge the tolls, in the form of oil pipelines that skirt the strait.
One headache is working out where they should go. Merely replicating SaudiÔÇÖs 1,200-kilometre, 7-million barrel-a-day pipeline to the port of Yanbu on the Red Sea would create new ÔÇőproblems. Gulf states would still have to get their product past the Bab el-Mandeb Strait near Yemen. The nearby territory is controlled by Iran-aligned Houthi rebels, raising the risk of further drone attacks on new and old pipelines.
Gabriel Collins, of Rice UniversityÔÇÖs Baker ÔÇőInstitute for Public Policy, has had a stab at a holistic workaround , opens new tab . He envisages twin pipelines with 56-inch diameters, each carrying 5 million barrels of daily supply, stretching 1,800 kilometres from southern Iraq through Kuwait and then along the coast to pick up oil from Saudi and the ÔÇîUAE. Terminating in Ôüátwo Omani ports, Duqm and Salalah, it would avoid both the Red Sea and Gulf chokepoints, meaning crude could flow freely east into the Indian Ocean to Gulf statesÔÇÖ main Asian customers. But it would take up to seven years to build ÔÇö and cost a lot.
Gulf states might have to fork out $18 billion alone on the construction cost of the pipelines and pumping stations to move the oil along them, Collins reckons. Add a further $12 billion in project development expenses and $7 billion headroom in the plausible event of the project taking longer, or hitting snags. He also assumes nearly $8 billion for new oil loading terminals at Duqm and Salalah, and $10 billion to fortify critical sections with C-RAM and Patriot anti-missile systems.
The overall price tag comes to $55 billion, which is roughly the same ÔÇőas the $54 billion low-end estimate for the present value of ÔÇő25 years of possible Hormuz toll payments. In other words, Ôüáthe pipeline projects could potentially pay for themselves over a generation by allowing the Gulf states to avoid Tehran's strait fees. That's surely worth doing, since the real tolls could be higher, and ending dependence on an adversary has incalculable value.
Even for hugely wealthy states like Saudi and the UAE, these numbers represent an expensive undertaking ÔÇö and an extremely unwelcome one, given all they would ÔÇődo is provide new infrastructure for the same fossil fuels they intend to increasingly pivot away from. Even if they take the plunge, they will still have to pay tolls ÔÇőbefore the pipelines become operational.
Still, it seems Ôüálogical for Saudi and its neighbours to get building. Over 80% of the burden of paying the tolls would likely fall on Gulf states, according to Bruegel research , opens new tab . They could split the cost of new pipelines in step with their intended usage.
Of course, there's another way to conceive of these numbers. Arguably they give Iran a maximum target for how much it could hope to raise. It should rationally set the tolls at a level that would be as high as possible without incentivising Gulf rivals to build the new pipelines.
And plenty could Ôüáchange in the ÔÇőnext seven years. U.S. President Donald Trump is now implementing a blockade of Iran-approved ships that are making it through Hormuz, and an intensified war that ÔÇőultimately unblocks Hormuz is still possible. Yet Iran has proved that it has the ability to wage asymmetric warfare around Hormuz, and the regime seems hard to budge. Even if Saudi and its neighbours pay $55 billion for pipelines that remain as a backup, they might still view that as a sound investment to ÔÇőcreate some geopolitical breathing room.
Follow George Hay on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Carmakers navigating the costly and tricky transition to battery storage systems
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Carmakers and battery companies facing a weak U.S. market for electric vehicles are scrambling to repurpose battery factories to make energy-storage systems to fuel AI's thirst for power instead. But converting plants to new types of batteries wonÔÇÖt be easy, ÔÇőnor will the demand for energy storage materialize fast enough to absorb a glut of unused factory space for EV batteries.
The expected surge in demand for electricity and energy storage comes at ÔÇîan opportune time for automakers like General Motors (GM.N) , opens new tab and Ford Motor (F.N) , opens new tab and their battery suppliers -- Asian manufacturers that include Japan's Panasonic Holdings (6752.T) , opens new tab , and South Korea's Samsung SDI and LG Energy Solution. These companies over the past decade spent or earmarked more than $100 billion toward battery factories to feed a U.S. electric-vehicle market that has been decimated by the Trump administrationÔÇÖs pro-fossil-fuel policies.
Stationary storage systems use lithium-ion battery cells ÔÇô similar to those in EVs ÔÇô that store power often generated by renewable sources like solar and wind, and release it during times of high demand ÔÇőor grid stress. U.S. demand is expected to grow partly to support data centers and cloud computing, which draw gobs of electricity.
But the predicted energy storage boom wouldnÔÇÖt come close to offsetting the bust in demand ÔÇőfor EV batteries, a Reuters review of projected U.S. battery-factory capacity this decade found. U.S. demand for EVs already had been falling short of automakers' original projections even before Ôüáa $7,500 consumer tax credit expired September 30, sending sales down more than 25% over the past six months.
Converting factories to the type of battery chemistry that many energy storage systems use will take time and yet more money, and ÔÇőrequire the companies to delve into a technology that today is dominated by China.
Bob Lee, head of North America for LG Energy Solution (LGES), a battery arm of Seoul-based LG Group, said his company is converting three of its factories in North ÔÇőAmerica to produce batteries for storage systems. He expects the battery business in the United States to continue to struggle with excess capacity, what he calls "fallout" from the EV bust.
ÔÇťLike any other industry that goes through a difficult period like this, I don't think it's going to be all rosy,ÔÇŁ he said.
Ford said it is spending $2 billion on a new battery-storage division over the next two years to "create a new, diversified and profitable revenue stream." GM's joint venture with LGES, called Ultium Cells, said last month it will convert an EV-battery ÔÇőplant in Tennessee to make battery cells for storage.
Demand for stationary batteries in North America will hit 76 gigawatt-hours this year, according to consultancy Benchmark Mineral Intelligence. But the auto industryÔÇÖs investment binge on ÔÇőEV battery capacity has left it with far more factory space than that: roughly 275 GWh. While storage demand is expected to nearly double over the next five years, to 125 GWh, that still wonÔÇÖt be enough to mop up the excess capacity installed ÔÇîfor the auto Ôüáindustry.
For example, LGES, which produces battery cells in North America at its own facilities and in joint ventures with General Motors, Honda and Hyundai, is converting factory space to be able to produce up to 50 GWh of batteries for storage a year by the end of this year. Still, thatÔÇÖs only about a third of the company's overall capacity in the region.
Most energy storage systems use a breed of batteries called lithium iron-phosphate, or LFP, which is cheaper than the nickel-heavy chemistry that is predominantly used for EV batteries in North America. Switching factories to LFP can take as long as 18 months and cost several hundred million dollars, battery executives told Reuters.
For U.S. battery makers, producing LFP is also complicated ÔÇőby ChinaÔÇÖs dominance of that technology and its supply chain. ÔÇőU.S.-based battery makers are working to cut their Ôüáreliance on Chinese materials to qualify for generous federal tax credits for domestic battery production that took effect during the Biden administration and were left in place under Trump, said Iola Hughes, BenchmarkÔÇÖs head of research. Producers must gradually phase out Chinese content in coming years to get the full tax breaks.
Trade barriers are another problem. U.S. tariffs on Chinese-made cathode and ÔÇőanode materials ÔÇô the two electrodes that ping-pong the electric charge back and forth inside a battery ÔÇô stand at 35%, according to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
The pivot to energy storage Ôüáby U.S. carmakers and battery companies has accelerated in recent months.
In March, the GM-LGES joint venture said it was spending $70 million and retraining workers to make battery cells for storage at its plant south of Nashville. Ford Motor in December disclosed it would repurpose some underused factory space in Kentucky to make storage batteries.
Traditional automakers are playing catch-up to Tesla. Elon Musk's company has spent about a decade developing its energy storage business, which has become one of the EV maker's fastest-growing divisions.
It was also more Ôüáprofitable than its ÔÇőEV business in 2025, with gross margins of about 30% compared to about 15% for its automotive business, excluding earnings from regulatory credits.
Deployments ÔÇőof TeslaÔÇÖs so-called Megapack storage units have surged, including $430 million in revenue last year from sales to MuskÔÇÖs xAI, an example of how AI-driven data-center demand is translating directly into battery orders.
Kurt Kelty, GM's battery chief and a former Tesla executive, told Reuters in January that the company is ÔÇőcommitted to building a battery manufacturing industry and supply chain in the U.S. Whether itÔÇÖs for EVs or storage systems, he said, ÔÇťit really doesnÔÇÖt matter.ÔÇŁ

item.title: Jamaat leader named as instigator, no arrests even after four days
item.description:
Police have not arrested anyone even four days after the killing of Pir Abdur Rahman, also known as Shamim, in Daulatpur, Kushtia. According to police, operations are ongoing to apprehend the accused.

On Monday night, the victimÔÇÖs elder brother, Fazlur Rahman, filed the murder case with Daulatpur police station. The main accused and alleged instigator in the case is local Jamaat leader Muhammad Khaza Ahmed (38). He is a former president of Kushtia district Chhatra Shibir and currently a member of the Daulatpur upazila Jamaat executive committee. He is son of Kalam Dafadar from Islmapur village under Philipnagar union.

The case statement names four individuals. The other three are Md Asaduzzaman (35), son of the late Ershad Ali from Hosenabad (Biswaspara) village in Daulatpur upazila, who is president of the Bangladesh Khelafat Majlis Daulatpur upazila unit; Rajib Mistri (45), son of Gazi Mistri from Darogar Mor area in southwest Philipnagar; and Md Shihab, a resident of Islmapur (Purba Para). In addition, 180 to 200 unidentified individuals have been accused.

Earlier, around 10:15 pm on Monday, police had verbally stated that only one person was named in the case.

Inspector Sheikh Md Ali Mortuza of Daulatpur police station has been assigned to investigate the case. Speaking today (Wednesday) afternoon, he told Prothom Alo, ÔÇťNo accused has been arrested yet. Hopefully, arrests will be made. Operations are ongoing.ÔÇŁ

In the case statement, Fazlur Rahman said that his younger brother, Abdur Rahman (57), had a Darbar Sharif at his home in Philipnagar. Around 2:45 pm on Saturday, his brother was at the Darbar when, allegedly on the orders of the main accused Khaza Ahmed, 180 to 200 individualsÔÇöincluding the named accusedÔÇöentered the premises armed with iron rods, sickles, machetes, knives, axes, bamboo sticks, and wooden batons. They vandalised doors and windows of the Darbar.

Rajib and others then went to the second floor and beat a man named Zobayer (31) indiscriminately with iron rods. After that, Rajib Mistri struck Abdur Rahman on the waist and then on the head with an iron rod with the intent to kill.

The complaint further states that unidentified attackers inflicted severe injuries on Abdur Rahman using sharp weapons, targeting his head, right jaw, lips, chin, left side of the back, and behind the right knee, leaving him critically wounded. They also beat him with bamboo sticks and wooden batons.

Hearing his screams, a caretaker of the Darbar, Jamiran, rushed in. The third accused, Asaduzzaman, allegedly attempted to strike her on the head with a sharp weapon; she blocked the blow with her left hand, suffering a deep injury to her wrist.

The complaint also alleges that the attackers vandalised and set fire to the Darbar Sharif, causing damages estimated at around Tk 2 million. They reportedly broke open a steel cabinet and stole Tk 500,000 in cash and four bhori of gold ornaments.

When contacted, Jamaat leader Muhammad Khaza Ahmed declined to comment.

On Tuesday night, the Daulatpur upazila Jamaat issued a press release regarding the incident. Signed by Amir Belal Uddin and Secretary Abdullah Al Noman, it stated that the Philipnagar incident was not part of any political activity. It claimed that none of those involved were leaders or activists of any political party and that the incident occurred spontaneously at the local level. It further alleged that a political group is falsely implicating Bangladesh Jamaat-e-Islami. The statement denied any involvement of Jamaat leaders or activists and demanded a proper investigation and punishment of the real culprits.

item.title: Gold prices rise by Tk 2,216 per bhori
item.description:
Gold prices have increased by Tk 2,216 per bhori again in the countryÔÇÖs market.

With this latest hike, the price of gold has once again crossed Tk 250,000 per bhori. The price of 22-carat gold, considered good quality, has increased by Tk 2,216 per bhori.

The Bangladesh Jewellers' Association (BAJUS) announced this in a press release today, Wednesday. The revised prices came into effect from 10:00am. Alongside gold, silver prices have also risen slightly.

Gold prices were last adjusted on Thursday last week, when the price of high-quality gold dropped by Tk 4,432 per bhori.

According to the new rates, the price of 22-carat gold now stands at Tk 250,193 per bhori. Meanwhile, 21-carat gold is priced at Tk 238,820 per bhori, 18-carat at Tk 204,703 per bhori, and traditional method gold at Tk 166,737 per bhori.

Before this latest increase, the prices were as follows: 22-carat gold at Tk 247,977 per bhori, 21-carat at Tk 236,721, 18-carat at Tk 202,895, and traditional gold at Tk 165,279 per bhori.

According to GoldPrice.com, as of Wednesday morning, gold was priced at $4,827 per ounce in the international market, which is $80 higher than when prices were last adjusted. In line with this increase, gold prices have been raised in the local market.

Overall, in the past 30 days, gold prices in the global market have declined by $177 per ounce. Prices have fallen following the outbreak of conflict involving Iran, Israel, and the United States, partly because gold had already been trading at elevated levels. In 2025, global gold prices rose by more than 70 per cent.

Meanwhile, silver prices have also increased. The price of 22-carat silver now stands at Tk 6,065 per bhori, up from Tk 5,715, marking an increase of Tk 350 per bhori.

In January, gold prices in the spot market reached close to $5,600 per ounce (31.1034768 grams). As a result, on 29 January, gold prices in the local market rose to Tk 286,000 per bhori ÔÇô the highest in the countryÔÇÖs history.

item.title: Ukraine retakes 50 sq km of territory in March, army chief says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Ukraine regained control of nearly 50 sq km (19 sq miles) of ÔÇőits territory from Russia in March, its army chief ÔÇîOleksandr Syrskyi said on Wednesday, building on its gains since the start of the year.
He added that with the change of weather conditions, Moscow's ÔÇőforces have stepped up their offensive operations and were ÔÇőconducting them along almost the entire 1,200-km front line.
Syrskyi Ôüásaid the area around the embattled eastern city of Pokrovsk, ÔÇőwhich Russia has been trying to capture fully since mid-2024, was ÔÇőamong the most intense places of fighting in March.
He also singled out Oleksandrivka, Kostiantynivka, and Lyman directions in the southeast and east as "the hottest" spots ÔÇőduring the month.
Ukrainian counterattacks in the southeast were helping to ÔÇődisrupt Russian efforts around Pokrovsk and the Russian spring offensive overall, military ÔÇîanalysts Ôüáhave said.
Syrskyi said last week that Ukraine has regained control of 480 sq km of territory since late January and President Volodymyr Zelenskiy earlier this month described the frontline situation as the best ÔÇőfor Ukraine since ÔÇőthe middle Ôüáof last year.
To reduce Russia's offensive capabilities, Ukrainian forces have kept up a high pace of ÔÇőstrikes on military, defence-industrial and other facilities in ÔÇőRussia, Syrskyi Ôüásaid.
In March, Ukraine struck 76 such targets including 15 facilities in the oil refining industry, he said.
Ukraine has intensified its attacks on ÔüáRussian ÔÇőports, refineries and fertiliser plants in ÔÇőan attempt to curb Moscow's revenue from commodities exports as the Iran war drove up global ÔÇőprices.

item.title: Hezbollah says Lebanon's talks with Israel widen national rift
item.description:
BEIRUT, April 15 (Reuters) - Hezbollah said on Wednesday the Lebanese government's decision to hold talks with Israel was "a national sin" that would widen divisions in Lebanon, underlining deep polarisation ÔÇőin the country as the Iran-backed group wages war with Israel.
Hezbollah lawmaker Hassan Fadlallah said ÔÇîTuesday's U.S.-mediated meeting between Lebanon's Washington ambassador and her Israeli counterpart did not reflect Lebanon's national identity or "the choices of its people".
The meeting hosted by U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio was the first ÔÇősuch direct contact in decades between the two countries that have remained in ÔÇőa state of war since Israel was established in 1948.
Both sides said Ôüáthe talks were positive, though ahead of the meeting, Israel had ruled out any ÔÇődiscussion of Lebanon's demand for a ceasefire in the war, which erupted on March 2 ÔÇőwhen Hezbollah opened fire in support of Tehran.
"Does the government not realize the danger of what it has undertaken? And does it understand that it has entered a wrong path that leads only to ÔÇőincreasing the rift among the Lebanese?" Fadlallah said.
"It has obtained nothing from the enemy ÔÇőexcept praise without achieving any demand," he said in a televised statement.
Tuesday's meeting took place at a ÔÇîcritical juncture Ôüáin the crisis in the Middle East, a week into a fragile ceasefire between the United States, Israel and Iran.
The wider conflict in the region began with U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28. Israel's offensive in Lebanon has killed more than 2,000 people and ÔÇőforced 1.2 million from ÔÇőtheir homes, according Ôüáto Lebanese authorities.
Fadlallah said his group wants a comprehensive ceasefire, rather than a return to near-daily Israeli strikes and assassinations as seen after ÔÇőit agreed to a previous ceasefire with Israel in November 2024.
The Lebanese ÔÇőstate has Ôüábeen seeking to disarm Hezbollah peacefully since the 2024 war. Any move by Lebanon to disarm it by force risks igniting conflict in a country shattered by civil war from 1975 Ôüáto ÔÇő1990. Moves against Hezbollah by a Western-backed government in ÔÇő2008 prompted a short civil war.
The current government banned Hezbollah's military wing after it opened fire on Israel last ÔÇőmonth.

item.title: Trump says he asked China's Xi not to give Iran weapons
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Donald Trump asked Chinese President Xi ÔÇőJinping in a letter not to give ÔÇîIran weapons, and Xi responded that China was not supplying Tehran, the U.S. president ÔÇőtold Fox Business Network in ÔÇőan interview that aired on Wednesday.
Trump, Ôüáin the interview taped on ÔÇőTuesday, did not say when the ÔÇőletters were exchanged. Last week, he threatened countries with an immediate 50% tariff if they ÔÇősupplied Iran with weapons.
"I wrote ÔÇőhim a letter asking him not to do ÔÇîthat, Ôüáand he wrote me a letter saying that, essentially, he's not doing that," Trump told FBN's "Mornings with ÔÇőMaria" program.
He ÔÇőalso said Ôüáhe did not expect shifts in the global ÔÇőoil market over the war ÔÇőon ÔüáIran and changes in Venezuela to impact the dynamics of his planned Ôüámeeting ÔÇőwith Xi next month . "He's somebody ÔÇőthat needs oil. We don't," Trump said.

item.title: Lula, Flavio Bolsonaro tied in potential election runoff, poll shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, April 15 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz ÔÇőInacio Lula da Silva and Senator ÔÇîFlavio Bolsonaro are statistically tied in a potential runoff in October's general election, ÔÇőwith the challenger leading Lula ÔÇőfor the first time within the Ôüásurvey's margin of error, a ÔÇőQuaest poll commissioned by brokerage Genial showed ÔÇőon Wednesday.
(This story has been refiled to fix a garble in the headline)

item.title: At least one killed in Turkey school shooting, NTV reports
item.description:
ANKARA, April 15 (Reuters) - At least one ÔÇőperson was ÔÇîkilled and six people were ÔÇőwounded in ÔÇőa shooting at a Ôüámiddle school ÔÇőin the ÔÇősouthern Turkish province of Kahramanmaras on ÔÇőWednesday, broadcaster ÔÇőNTV reported citing the ÔÇîlocal Ôüágovernor, marking the second such attack in ÔÇőas ÔÇőmany Ôüádays.
No other details were ÔÇőimmediately ÔÇőavailable. ÔüáSchool shootings are very rare Ôüáin ÔÇőTurkey.

item.title: Erdogan says Turkey working to extend Iran ceasefire, continue talks
item.description:
ANKARA, April 15 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan said on Wednesday that Ankara was working to extend a ceasefire between ÔÇőthe U.S. and Iran, ease tensions, and ensure the continuation of ÔÇîtalks, and added Turkey was hopeful of negotiations despite stumbling blocks.
Turkey, a neighbour of Iran, has been in close touch with the U.S., Iran, and mediator Pakistan. It ÔÇőhas repeatedly called for an end to the war that it ÔÇősays is unjustified.
Speaking to lawmakers in parliament, Erdogan said Israel's Ôüáattacks in Lebanon were harming hopes for peace, and added the window of ÔÇőopportunity created by the ceasefire must be seized.
"Statements by the sides show ÔÇőthat, while the negotiation table has not been toppled, they have reached a road bump on the nuclear issue," he said, adding tensions were also flaring again over the Strait ÔÇőof Hormuz.
"We are conveying the necessary suggestions and carrying out initiatives ÔÇőto ease tensions, extend the ceasefire, and maintain talks. There can be no negotiating with ÔÇîclenched Ôüáfists," Erdogan added.
U.S. President Donald Trump said talks with Iran could soon resume and end in a deal, after weekend negotiations in Pakistan ended with no breakthrough, while telling the world to watch out for an "amazing two ÔÇődays".
A Turkish diplomatic ÔÇősource has Ôüásaid foreign ministers from Turkey, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, and Egypt will meet on the margins of a diplomacy forum ÔÇőin the southern Turkish province of Antalya at the ÔÇőweekend. Pakistan's ÔüáPrime Minister Shehbaz Sharif is also due to attend .
"There may be difficulties and complicated issues that will take a long time to solve, but when the Ôüáfocus ÔÇőis on the benefits of peace, and action ÔÇőis taken with a long-term perspective, the majority of these can be resolved," Erdogan said.

item.title: French government's proposed cap on retail margins at gas stations faces stiff industry opposition
item.description:
PARIS, April 15 (Reuters) - France's proposal to cap profit margins on transport fuels sold throughout the country's service stations prompted an immediate backlash from the distribution industry, according to a lobbying letter seen by Reuters on Wednesday.
The French government said on Tuesday it had ÔÇősubmitted for consultation a draft decree that would fix prices to ensure fuel distributors did ÔÇőnot earn margins higher than those seen in January and February, based on a Ôüásliding five-day average of wholesale prices at Rotterdam.
It is one of a host of measures European countries ÔÇőare considering from Dublin to Bucharest to ease consumer pain from record high oil and fuel prices due to disruptions ÔÇőcaused by the war in Iran.
In a letter to the prime minister dated April 14, France's Federation of Commerce and Distribution (FCD) argued the decree would force distributors operating on margins of 1 to 2 eurocents per litre to sell at a ÔÇőloss due to the lack of real-time price updates ÔÇö and leave untouched the vertically integrated oil companies who ÔÇőthey say are earning much larger profits from the crisis.
The letter, which has not been made public, was signed by ÔÇîCarrefour (CARR.PA) , opens new tab ÔüáCEO Alexandre Bompard, Cooperative U CEO Dominique Schelcher, Mouvement E. Leclerc Executive Committee Chair Philippe Michaud, Groupement Les Mousquetaires President Thierry Cotillard, and Barthelemy Guislain, president of the Mulliez Family Association, which owns the Auchan supermarket chain.
Together the five companies represent more than a third of France's service stations, according to Reuters calculations.
"This decree ÔÇőwould make large distribution chains ÔÇőthe convenient variable of Ôüáadjustment of a crisis it has not created... while imposing absolutely nothing on the refiner-distributors who have captured considerable gross margins since the beginning of the ÔÇőconflict in Iran," the letter reads.
Though not named in the letter, French oil ÔÇőmajor TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab owns Ôüáthree of France's six oil refineries and a quarter of the country's service stations.
TotalEnergies is expected to announce significant trading profits on Thursday in its first-quarter earnings snapshot from rising oil prices and higher refining margins.
TotalEnergies declined Ôüáto comment ÔÇőon the letter, but said it was the only distributor ÔÇőin France to voluntarily cap prices at the pump.
Canadian energy group North Atlantic also recently acquired the French Esso refinery in Gravenchon, which supplies about ÔÇő7% of French fuel stations.

item.title: Central Asia, Afghanistan crank up Russian fuel imports as MidEast supplies dry up
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Central Asia and Afghanistan increased fuel imports from Russia and Belarus by ÔÇőrail by more than 50% in the ÔÇîfirst quarter as Moscow diverts energy flows from Europe and the Iran war curbs deliveries from the ÔÇőMiddle East, traders said on Wednesday.
The ÔÇőtraders said supplies to the region in ÔüáJanuary-March increased to 3.347 million metric tons.
Since ÔÇőthe European Union introduced an embargo on ÔÇőRussia's oil products in February 2023, the region has become the main export market for Russian fuel.
While Russia ÔÇőhas banned gasoline exports until the end ÔÇőof July, many Central Asian countries, with which Russia ÔÇîhas Ôüáinter-governmental agreements on fuel supplies, are exempt from the restrictions.
Mongolia is the biggest Russian fuel importer in the region. Supplies to the ÔÇőcountry in ÔÇőthe first Ôüáquarter rose by 29% year on year to 840,000 tons.
Afghanistan is ÔÇőnot exempt from Russia's gasoline export ÔÇőban, Ôüábut Belarus can continue supplies.
The Taliban-ruled country's imports from Russia and Belarus jumped fourfold in Ôüáthe ÔÇőfirst quarter from the same ÔÇőperiod in 2025 to 530,000 tons, including 231,000 tons ÔÇőof gasoline.

item.title: Goldman Sachs flags twoÔÇĹway risks to their 2026 oil price outlook
item.description: April 15 (Reuters) - Goldman Sachs on Tuesday flagged both upside and downside risks to its average 2026 crude forecasts for Brent/WTI at $83/78 per barrel, citing growing ÔÇőuncertainty around Middle East developments and oil flows through the Strait ÔÇîof Hormuz.

item.title: Botswana has not requested stake in Lobito refinery, Angola's Sonangol says
item.description:
CAPE TOWN, April 15 (Reuters) - State-owned oil firm Sonangol has not ÔÇőreceived any formal communication from Botswana signalling its desire to acquire a stake in ÔÇîAngola's new $6.6 billion Lobito refinery, a senior company executive said late on Tuesday.
Angola has been seeking partners to help finance the delayed refinery ÔÇö the country's largest and aimed at cutting its reliance on imported fuel ÔÇö with financing shortfalls ÔÇőand potential partnerships drawing interest from neighbours.
Widespread media reports quoting Botswana's energy minister, Bogolo Joy ÔÇőKenewendo, in parliament on March 27, suggested that the government was offered and Ôüáis weighing options to secure a 30% shareholding in the 200,000 barrel per day (bpd) plant once ÔÇőit comes online.
Botswana's ministry of energy did not immediately respond to multiple requests for comment from Reuters.
The ÔÇőinformation from the media was surprising, Joaquim Kiteculo, CEO of Sonangol's refining division, told Reuters on the sidelines of an energy conference in Cape Town, adding that it was Zambia that had aimed to join the Lobito project ÔÇősince the beginning.
"It was our first time to hear that Botswana is interested," Kiteculo said.
Angola and ÔÇőZambia have a memorandum of understanding covering participation in the refinery. Botswana, a land-locked country, is keen to boost storage ÔÇîcapacity and Ôüádiversify its fuel supplies as it also considers partnering with Namibia on their proposed first refinery.
Angola is open to partnerships with new investors but will retain a 51% majority in any restructuring, Kiteculo said.
A source in Angola's energy ministry said the refinery issue would likely be discussed once President Dumo ÔÇőBoko returns to Angola.
Lobito refinery, part of Angolan plans to reduce its dependency on imported petroleum products, has been delayed for years due to the lack of financing.
Angola's ÔÇőmines and petroleum minister is currently in China with a senior Sonangol delegation, ÔÇőseeking to drum Ôüáup support to overcome a $4.8 billion funding shortfall for Lobito.
"I would say, in the first stage, it is going to be $2.2 billion and later, an additional $2.6, but the situation currently remains the same as Ôüábefore," Kiteculo ÔÇősaid of ongoing talks.
Sonangol, which has already spent $1.4 billion of its ÔÇőown capital for road and water infrastructure in the first phase of construction, will continue to invest with or without partners ÔÇőuntil the project is completed, he said.

item.title: Italy's Enel to hire 1,000 workers at power distribution unit, unions say
item.description: MILAN, April 15 (Reuters) - Italy's biggest utility Enel (ENEI.MI) , opens new tab will hire 1,000 ÔÇőnew workers at its ÔÇîpower distribution unit EÔÇĹDistribuzione under an agreement with trade unions ÔÇőaimed at ending a ÔÇőtwoÔÇĹyear labour dispute, unions said Ôüáon Wednesday.

item.title: Australia to raise defence spending to 3% of GDP by 2033
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 15 (Reuters) - Australia on Wednesday said it would ÔÇîraise defence spending to 3% of gross domestic product by 2033 from about 2% currently, in response to the most "complex and threatening" circumstances since World War Two.
The expansion would be achieved through ÔÇőan additional A$14 billion ($10 billion) in expenditure over four years and A$53 ÔÇőbillion over the next decade, the government said.
Australia has been looking to Ôüádevelop its defence capabilities amid concerns over rising global geopolitical tensions and China's ÔÇőtussle with the U.S. for influence in the Indo-Pacific.
The Trump administration in the U.S. has ÔÇőcalled on Canberra to lift defence spending to 3.5% of GDP.
Australia's centre-left Labor government will outline the spending in the Integrated Investment Program due to be released on Thursday, with the biennial ÔÇőNational Defence Strategy.
"Australia faces its most complex and threatening strategic circumstances since the end ÔÇőof World War Two," Defence Minister Richard Marles is expected to say in a speech in ÔÇîCanberra Ôüáon Thursday to launch the latest strategy.
"International norms that once constrained the use of force and military coercion continue to erode."
"The result is that we are now seeing the biggest peacetime increase in defence spending in our nation's history."
Australia's defence spending currently sits ÔÇőat around 2% ÔÇőof GDP.
The government has Ôüáalready committed an additional A$50 billion in spending over the next decade to reach 2.33% in 2033-34.
Taken together, it means Prime ÔÇőMinister Anthony Albanese's government has committed an additional A$117 billion ÔÇőin spending Ôüáover the next decade.
The government said the money would be spent on "capabilities that increase the ADF's (Australian Defence Force) ability to deter and respond to current and emerging threats, while also Ôüáinvesting ÔÇőin the longer term to build a more self-reliant ÔÇőADF."
Australia will pay for the expanded expenditure through bigger defence appropriations and accessing private capital, Marles is ÔÇőexpected to say.
($1 = 1.4000 Australian dollars)

item.title: South African politician Malema back in court as prosecutors seek 15-year prison sentence
item.description:
KUGOMPO CITY, South Africa, April 15 (Reuters) - South African opposition politician Julius Malema was back in court on Wednesday, as prosecutors pushed for ÔÇőhim to be given the maximum 15-year prison sentence for ÔÇîfiring a rifle in the air at a rally.
Malema, one of South Africa's most prominent politicians, was convicted last year of charges, including unlawful possession of a firearm ÔÇőand discharging a weapon in a public place over the ÔÇő2018 incident.
The 45-year-old leader of the fourth-biggest party in parliament, Ôüáthe far-left Economic Freedom Fighters, had pleaded not guilty, arguing the ÔÇőgun was a toy.
A prison term of more than 12 months, if ÔÇőconfirmed after all appeals, would bar Malema from serving as a lawmaker.
That would be a major setback to EFF, which has strong support among young South Africans frustrated ÔÇőby the racial inequality that has persisted since the end of ÔÇőwhite minority rule in 1994.
The state ÔÇîprosecutor Ôüátold a magistrate's court in KuGompo City, formerly East London, that it would set a bad precedent if Malema were to get off without jail time.
"The accused is a political leader with a huge following ... young ÔÇőpeople emulate this ÔÇőkind of behaviour," Ôüáprosecutor Joel Cesar said. "He's a member of parliament, a lawmaker, but he breaks the law."
Malema's lawyers argued he ÔÇődid not intend to cause any harm by firing ÔÇőthe Ôüágun in a celebratory gesture, and called for a more lenient sentence such as a fine.
The court adjourned to Thursday.
Addressing hundreds of his supporters from Ôüáa ÔÇőpodium set up outside the court building, Malema ÔÇősaid he would appeal whatever sentence he was given.

item.title: Dubai's landmark Burj Al Arab hotel will shut for 18-month refurb amid tourism decline
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Luxury hotel Burj Al Arab in Dubai will shut during a major 18-month renovation, a ÔÇőstaff member confirmed on Wednesday, its first since ÔÇîopening in 1999 and at a time when tourism in the region has slowed due to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.
The ÔÇőhotel's owner Jumeirah said in a statement on ÔÇőTuesday the work would be carried out in Ôüáphases over some 18 months and would be ÔÇőled by Paris-based interior architect Tristan Auer. It did ÔÇőnot specify that the property would be closed during the renovation.
The staff member said the hotel is offering alternative accommodation ÔÇőin nearby hotels to guests with bookings during ÔÇőthe work. The period of closure is subject to change, the ÔÇîperson Ôüásaid.
The sail-shaped hotel, one of Dubai's best-known landmarks and the flagship property of the Jumeirah group, suffered some damage when debris from an interception of an Iranian ÔÇődrone attack hit ÔÇőits facade Ôüáin early March.
The "long-awaited" work is not linked with the incident in March, the ÔÇőemployee said. The United Arab Emirates-based Jumeirah did ÔÇőnot Ôüálink the project to the war in its statement.
The timing, however, is notable because the conflict has hurt Ôüátravel to ÔÇőDubai, with flight disruptions affecting the ÔÇőUAE and luxury groups warning of pressure on profits as visitor demand weakens.

item.title: Peru seeks second candidate for presidential runoff, leftist Sanchez gains ground
item.description:
LIMA, April 15 (Reuters) - Peru entered on Wednesday its fourth day of vote counting ÔÇőwith no clear second candidate to face conservative ÔÇîleader Keiko Fujimori in a presidential runoff, official data showed with about 90% of ballots counted.
Left-wing congressman, Roberto Sanchez, and ÔÇőright-wing former Lima mayor, Rafael Lopez Aliaga, are ÔÇővying for second place with about 12% of Ôüáthe votes, followed closely by center-left candidate Jorge Nieto ÔÇőwith roughly 11%, Peru's electoral body, ONPE, said.
Sanchez moved ÔÇőinto second place in the tight election , opens new tab overnight, while Fujimori, a former congresswoman and the daughter of late former President Alberto Fujimori, remained ÔÇőin first place with 16.9% of the vote ÔÇőin Sunday's election, according to the official tally.
She is set to ÔÇîadvance Ôüáto a runoff expected to occur on June 7, in her fourth bid for the presidency of the Andean country. Her opponent remains unclear, with three candidates neck ÔÇőand neck ÔÇőin the vote Ôüácount, while none of them was able to achieve the 50% of the ÔÇővotes needed to win outright.
As counting continues, allegations ÔÇőof Ôüáfraud have started to spread more widely. Lopez Aliaga and Sanchez have shown concerns about the integrity of the Ôüáprocess ÔÇőwith Lopez Aliaga sharing early ÔÇőWednesday a post that called for Keiko to request the proceedings to ÔÇőbe annulled.

item.title: Four killed, 20 wounded in school shooting in southern Turkey, local governor says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 15 (Reuters) - Four people were killed ÔÇőand 20 others ÔÇîwere wounded when a middle school ÔÇőstudent opened ÔÇőfire at a school Ôüáin the ÔÇőTurkey's southeastern province ÔÇőof Kahramanmaras on Wednesday, Governor Mukerrem Unluer told ÔÇőreporters.
Unluer said ÔÇőa teacher was among ÔÇîthose Ôüákilled, and added the shooter was a student ÔÇőwho used ÔÇőhis Ôüáfather's weapons, concealed in ÔÇőa backpack, ÔÇőto Ôüácarry out the attack. The shooter Ôüáalso ÔÇődied, ÔÇőhe added.

item.title: Bosnia selects US investor for gas link with Croatia to cut reliance on Russia
item.description:
SARAJEVO, April 15 (Reuters) - A regional parliament in Bosnia and ÔÇîHerzegovina on Wednesday named U.S. company AAFS Infrastructure and Energy LLC as the main investor and leader of a long-delayed project to build a natural gas pipeline with Croatia that is seen as an ÔÇőalternative to Russian gas.
Both houses of the Bosniak-Croat Federation's parliament agreed to amend ÔÇőa law regulating the Southern Interconnection gas pipeline project, which will bring ÔüáU.S. natural gas to Bosnia from a liquefied natural gas terminal on the Croatian ÔÇőisland of Krk.
The Federation is one of two autonomous regions making up postwar Bosnia. The ÔÇőother is the Serb Republic.
Sarajevo-based state company BH Gas, which had previously been appointed to lead the project, was removed from the legislation which Croatian deputies in parliament's upper house blocked for years, insisting ÔÇőon the establishment of a new transmission system operator in the Croat-dominated part of ÔÇőwestern and southern Bosnia, through which the pipeline will pass.
The Federation government previously said it was necessary ÔÇîto Ôüáamend the legislation in order to diversify energy sources and avoid a possible energy crisis when the European Union halts Russian gas deliveries from 2028.
AAFS' director in Sarajevo, Amir Bekan, said the total value of the company's investment in Bosnia came to around $1.5 billion, ÔÇőwith most of the ÔÇőamount accounted for Ôüáby the pipeline, but with around $300 million for the modernisation of the airports in Sarajevo and Mostar.
Natural gas accounts for up to ÔÇő8% of Bosnia's energy requirement and is supplied solely by Russia ÔÇővia Serbia Ôüáthrough the TurkStream pipeline as the country does not have any reserves of its own.
The government will now sign a direct agreement with AAFS. It is also expected to sign an Ôüáagreement on ÔÇőthe pipeline with Croatia at the end of ÔÇőApril.
Observers have cautioned that Bosnia's complex and fragmented legal system may slow down procedures, especially those related to the ÔÇőexpropriation of state-owned land on the pipeline's route.

item.title: Slovak refiner says no deal yet on Adria pipeline tests
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Slovak oil refiner Slovnaft said on Wednesday it had still not reached an agreement with Croatia over tests ÔÇőto see if the Adria oil pipeline can carry enough supplies for Hungary ÔÇîand Slovakia, although existing shipments had enabled it to boost production.
The pipeline from Croatia is an alternative oil route to Slovakia and Hungary, and its importance has grown with the Druzhba pipeline - which carries Russian crude and ÔÇőruns through Ukraine - out of operation since the end of January.
Supplies through Adria have ÔÇőhelped Slovnaft - a unit of Hungarian oil and gas group MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab - to Ôüáramp up processing after a drop caused by the Druzhba outage, Slovnaft's Chief Executive Gabriel ÔÇőSzabo told a televised press conference.
He said that tests of the Adria pipeline, including in different ÔÇőweather conditions, were needed to discover whether it had sufficient capacity to serve the two countries.
MOL and Croatian pipeline operator JANAF had previously agreed on the testing to start in March, according to the Hungarian company, but there ÔÇőwere still disagreements over details, Szabo said.
Slovnaft had to ÔÇőcut output and borrow from national crude oil reserves after the Druzhba outage, which Ukraine says was caused ÔÇîby a ÔüáRussian attack on the pipeline in late January.
Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Tuesday that Druzhba would be restarted by the end of April.
Slovnaft has since returned the borrowed crude thanks to shipments of seaborne non-Russian oil through the Adria and the 6-million-metric-ton-per-year refinery, which exports diesel to ÔÇőneighbouring countries, is now ÔÇőrunning near 100% capacity, ÔüáSzabo said.
"We are going at 90 if not 100%, but the technological capacity is limited by the fact that we have to process other ÔÇőcrudes than Russian," Szabo said, without giving details on exact volumes.
He ÔÇősaid supplies to ÔüáSlovnaft were also enabled by an outage at another MOL refinery in Hungary following a fire, which limited the amounts required through Adria.
Slovnaft has been investing in technological changes to use exclusively non-Russian Ôüáoil ÔÇőblends from 2027.
The European Union plans to wean itself completely ÔÇőoff Russian oil, though Hungary and Slovakia have objected to the plans and the European Commission has delayed submitting a legal ÔÇőproposal on the matter.

DU English department alumni reunion on 2 May

item.title: 'I stood in line at 1am and got fuel at 2pm'
item.description:
ÔÇťI stood in line at 1am, and now at 2pm I finally got fuel. The suffering is endless. I couldnÔÇÖt eat properly, and standing in the sun for so long has made me sick,ÔÇŁ said motorcyclist Al Amin Hossain.

This correspondent spoke to him today, Wednesday at Trust Filling Station in Tejgaon. At around 2:00pm, he was seen collecting fuel using the Fuel Pass app.

He said he obtained fuel through the app and would not be able to purchase fuel again for another week. However, this fuel would cover the entire week. To get it, he had to queue from 1:00am the previous night.

At that time, the line of motorcycles stretched all the way to the Mohakhali rail gate. Slowly, following the queue, it took him from night to morning, and then from morning to afternoon to finally reach the station.

Al Amin said, ÔÇťA few of my friends and I were standing in line. We never imagined it would take this long. The suffering has been immense. But thereÔÇÖs nothing we can doÔÇöwe need fuel to keep moving.ÔÇŁ

Long queues of vehicles were seen outside Trust Filling Station in Tejgaon. At around 1:30pm, 512 motorcycles and 407 private cars were waiting in line.

Meanwhile, several filling stations in Mohakhali were found closed due to a lack of fuel. At around 11:00am, Royal Filling Station had no fuel and remained shut. An official, Swadhin Ahmed, said they had been out of fuel for four days. A vehicle had been sent to the depot again today, but it returned empty.

Another station in Mohakhali, SR Enterprise, was also closed around 11:30am due to a shortage. Employee Md Habib said, ÔÇťWeÔÇÖve had no fuel for three days. A vehicle has gone to collect fuel todayÔÇöif it returns, we can resume sales.ÔÇŁ

On the road from Mohakhali to Jahangir Gate, a long line of motorcycles was seen outside Eureka Enterprise Filling Station. Around noon, Tashofa Filling Station at Mohakhali intersection was also closed due to a lack of fuel.

Imran Hossain, manager of the filling station, told Prothom Alo that the government is supplying the same amount of fuel as in April 2025, but demand has surged. Previously, 9,000 liters of octane lasted three days; now it runs out within a few hours, forcing the station to remain closed for the rest of the time.

M/S Sohag Filling Station was also closed due to the shortage. Cashier Ranjan Sutradhar said they received 4,500 liters of octane on 12 April, which was sold out the same day. Today, although they managed to get petrol, they did not receive octane, so they could not operate.

Kamrul Hasan, who came to Eureka Enterprise Filling Station around 12:30pm after taking leave from work, said, ÔÇťIÔÇÖve been standing in the sun for two hours after taking leave from the office just to get fuel. The suffering is unbearable. But thereÔÇÖs nothing we can doÔÇöif we want fuel, we have to endure this.ÔÇŁ

He added that the situation has worsened because nearby stations remain closed.

ÔÇťThis station also didnÔÇÖt have fuel earlier; it just arrived. If other stations were open, the suffering wouldnÔÇÖt be this severe.ÔÇŁ

Due to the war in the Middle East involving Iran and the United StatesÔÇôIsrael, global fuel markets have become unstable, leading to supply shortages. The impact has reached Bangladesh as well. Long queues at filling stations are growing longer, and customers say the extreme heat is making the ordeal even worse.

item.title: Upazila boat ambulance lies abandoned, never put into service
item.description:
The upazilaÔÇÖs only boat ambulance, launched in 2022 to ensure the quick transport of patients to hospitals via waterways, now lies abandoned on the bank of a canal.

Though it appears functional from the outside, it has no engine, and other essential parts have been stolen.

In the four years since its inauguration, it has not been used even once to carry patients.

The ambulance is lying in a derelict state beside the Centre Canal near Alexander Bazar in Ramgati upazila of Lakshmipur.

The boat ambulance, named Swapnojatra, was purchased in 2022 for people living in the remote char areas of the upazila. Around Tk 300,000 was spent on it.

According to sources at the upazila administration, the ambulance was purchased under an initiative by the district administration. Although it was inaugurated in 2022, no driver was recruited at the time. As a result, while lying unused, many of its parts were stolen.

Char Abdullah union parishad Chairman Kamal Uddin Monju said that due to various constraints, including the lack of a driver, the ambulance could not be made operational from the outset. As a result, people in the char areas are being deprived of their basic right to medical services.

Residents frequently suffer due to the absence of the boat ambulance service. On 8 April this year, a family in Char Abdullah faced such a situation. A child, Md Mamun, had been suffering from severe abdominal pain since morning.

Medicines from nearby primary healthcare centres did not help. Neighbours then advised taking him to a hospital. However, no boat was available for four hours. Eventually, a small boat was found, and he was taken to the upazila health complex.

Zakir HossainThe boat ambulance has never been used. I have not seen it carry a single patient even for a day

Describing the incident, neighbour Saiful Islam said MamunÔÇÖs condition suddenly became criticalÔÇöhe was not even able to respond. Everyone became anxious, but with no transport available, the family was helpless. After much difficulty, he was finally taken to the hospital and is now doing relatively well.

MamunÔÇÖs father, Zakir Hossain, who earns his livelihood by fishing in the river, said, ÔÇťThe boat ambulance has never been of any use to people. I have not seen it carry a single patient even for a day.ÔÇŁ

Local residents said several thousand people live in Char Abdullah, Chargazaria, and nearby char areas of Ramgati. Their communication depends entirely on waterways. During the monsoon, connectivity between villages becomes almost completely cut off. In emergencies, traveling to Ramgati costs Tk 4,000 to Tk 5,000 by rented trawler.

Locals allege that due to poor planning and lack of supervision, the boat ambulance initiative failed from the beginning. As a result, people in char areas are being deprived of emergency healthcare. Although it was not used as an ambulance, local Awami League leaders initially used the boat. Following criticism, the then Upazila Nirbahi Officer (UNO) recovered it and left it in the canal.

Upazila Nirbahi Officer Nilufa Yasmin said the matter has come to their attention. They will investigate why it has remained nonfunctional for so long and will take necessary steps quickly to ensure that people in char areas are not deprived of emergency medical services.
