item.title: Pope Leo says world needs message of peace and coexistence
item.description:
ABOARD THE PAPAL FLIGHT, April 15 (Reuters) - Pope ÔÇőLeo said on Wednesday ÔÇîthat the world needs to hear ÔÇőa message of ÔÇőpeace and coexistence, in Ôüáremarks hours after ÔÇőU.S. President Donald ÔÇőTrump's latest social media attack against the pontiff.
Speaking on ÔÇőhis flight from ÔÇőCameroon to Algeria for the ÔÇîsecond Ôüáleg of the tour, the first U.S. pontiff urged ÔÇőrespect ÔÇőfor Ôüáall the peoples of the ÔÇőworld and ÔÇősaid Ôüáhis tour has shown the importance of Ôüápursuing ÔÇődialogue between ÔÇődifferent communities.

item.title: Kenyan court fines and jails Chinese man in ant-smuggling case
item.description:
NAIROBI, April 15 (Reuters) - A Kenyan court on Wednesday ordered a ÔÇîChinese man to pay a fine of 1 million shillings ($7,746) and gave him a 12-month jail term for trying to smuggle live ants out of the country.
The ÔÇőmagistrate in the case said a stiff sentence was needed ÔÇőas a deterrent given a spate of cases in Kenya Ôüáof ant-trafficking.
It serves markets, such as China, where enthusiasts have paid ÔÇőlarge sums to maintain ant colonies in large transparent vessels known as ÔÇőformicariums that allow them to study the species' complex social structures and behaviours.
Chinese national Zhang Kequn was arrested last month at Nairobi's main international airport with more than ÔÇő2,200 live garden ants in his luggage.
Zhang's lawyer said he would ÔÇőappeal against his sentence.
He initially pleaded not guilty to charges including dealing in live ÔÇîwildlife Ôüáspecies but later changed his plea to guilty.
"Noting the increasing and rising cases of dealing in large quantities of garden ants and the negative ecological side effects of massive harvesting, there is a need for ÔÇőa stiff deterrent," ÔÇőmagistrate Irene Gichobi Ôüásaid.
A Kenyan man, Charles Mwangi, was also charged in the case, accused of supplying the ants to Zhang.
Mwangi has ÔÇőpleaded not guilty and is out on bail. His ÔÇőcase Ôüáwas not before the court on Wednesday.
Last year four men were fined 1 million shillings each for trying to traffic thousands of ants. Wildlife experts said Ôüáat ÔÇőthe time that the case signalled a ÔÇőshift in biopiracy from trophies like elephant ivory to lesser-known species.
($1 = 129.1000 Kenyan shillings)

item.title: Energean hopes for major discovery off western Greece, says CEO
item.description:
ATHENS, April 15 (Reuters) - Oil and gas producer Energean (ENOG.L) , opens new tab will aim for a big discovery of hydrocarbons off western Greece ÔÇőwhere the first test drilling could take place ÔÇîas early as next year, CEO Mathios Rigas said on Wednesday.
ExxonMobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab last year joined Energean and Greece's biggest oil refiner Helleniq Energy (HEPr.AT) , opens new tab to ÔÇőexplore for natural gas in the Ionian Sea ÔÇőas the United States seeks a growing role Ôüáin helping Europe replace much of the Russian gas ÔÇőfollowing Moscow's invasion of Ukraine.
"We are aiming for a big ÔÇődiscovery but it will mean nothing if the first drilling is not successful," said Rigas in a ceremony in Athens where Energean ÔÇőmandated Sweden's Stena Drilling to drill the first well.
The ÔÇődrilling in Block 2, an area extending across 1,000 square kilometres, ÔÇîis Ôüáthe first deep-sea test drilling in Greece in almost 40 years and could take place in February 2027 once permits are secured, Rigas said.
The "Asopos" structure could carry recoverable deposits ÔÇőof gas ÔÇőand oil Ôüáof 6-7 trillion cubic feet and the likelihood of success was about 16%, he added.
"The ÔÇőchances are very good," he said, adding ÔÇőthat Ôüáthe development would involve a floating offshore facility to link subsea wells to Greece and Italy.
Greece now produces only very Ôüásmall ÔÇőquantities of oil and any discovery ÔÇőof gas will help the country cover both domestic demand and start ÔÇőexporting to Europe.

item.title: Oilfield services firm NOV says Middle East disruptions to weigh on first quarter
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - NOV (NOV.N) , opens new tab said on Wednesday its first-quarter revenue and earnings will ÔÇőcome in below its prior outlook due to delays in ÔÇîequipment deliveries and higher logistics costs linked to the war in the Middle East.
Shares of the oilfield equipment and services provider fell nearly 3% in premarket trading.
The ÔÇőcompany estimated the conflict reduced revenue by around $54 million and ÔÇőadjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation (EBITDA) by about $32 Ôüámillion.
"These challenges disproportionately affected quarter-end deliveries of capital equipment and products, ÔÇőincluding spare parts, in the region, while our more service-oriented offerings were ÔÇőless affected," said CEO Jose Bayardo.
"Higher shipping and freight costs and reduced absorption in our manufacturing facilities further amplified decremental margins."
Rising security risks and damage to energy infrastructure ÔÇőhave driven drilling and oilfield activity sharply lower across the Middle ÔÇőEast, cutting demand for oilfield services and equipment in one of the world's top ÔÇîenergy-producing Ôüáregions.
Industry bellwether SLB (SLB.N) , opens new tab last month said the conflict would reduce its first-quarter earnings by about 6 cents to 9 cents per share and push revenue below expectations.
Major energy companies are also taking precautionary steps. ExxonMobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab has evacuated non-essential ÔÇőstaff from some ÔÇőMiddle East operations Ôüáafter the war broke out between Iran and U.S.-Israel.
NOV had previously forecast first-quarter adjusted EBITDA in the range ÔÇőof $200 million to $225 million and its revenue to ÔÇődecline 1% Ôüáto 3% from a year earlier.
It now expects to report revenue of about $2.05 billion for the quarter, with operating profit of $47 million and adjusted ÔüáEBITDA of $177 ÔÇőmillion.
The company said none of its facilities ÔÇőin the region were damaged and it is working to mitigate delays and rising costs.

item.title: Russia's Novatek says its gas output up 3% y/y in Q1
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia's largest liquefied ÔÇőnatural gas ÔÇîproducer Novatek (NVTK.MM) , opens new tab said on Wednesday ÔÇőits first-quarter ÔÇőnatural gas production edged Ôüáup by ÔÇő3.1% from ÔÇőthe same period in 2025 to ÔÇő22.03 billion ÔÇőcubic metres.
It also said ÔÇîits Ôüáoutput of oil and gas condensate increased ÔÇőby ÔÇő4.2% Ôüáto 3.62 million tons ÔÇőin ÔÇőthe ÔüáJanuary - March quarter.

Tarique Rahman makes TIMEÔÇÖs list of 100 most influential people
item.description:
Prime Minister Tarique Rahman has been named in the TIME magazineÔÇÖs list of the 100 Most Influential People in the World for 2026, in recognition of his leadership, courage and contribution to restoration of democracy.

The list published by the New York-based weekly on Wednesday also contains the names of US President Donald Trump, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer, Pope Leo XIV, Nepal's Prime Minister Balendra Shah and New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

In his profile, TIME editor-at-large Charlie Campbell said Tarique Rahman was living a carefree life of exile in leafy southwest London just a few months ago. ÔÇťBut the 2024 ousting of BangladeshÔÇÖs autocratic Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina propelled the 57-year-old scion from opposition agitator to national leader inÔÇ»waiting ÔÇô a destiny he fulfilled in February by winning an electoral landslide after 17 years estranged from his homeland.ÔÇŁ

It was a victory that meant Tarique Rahman had followed in the footsteps of his mother Begum Khaleda Zia, who was BangladeshÔÇÖs first female Prime Minister and who passed away just five days after his return to Dhaka, he said. ÔÇťHis grief was still raw when Rahman sat down with TIME in January, though he vowed to channel it into unifying his country of 175 million, while getting South AsiaÔÇÖs second biggest economy buzzing again.

Bangladesh is beset by high inflation and youth unemployment, and relations with regional superpower India have reached a historic nadir. All require swift remedy.ÔÇŁ

Sharing the information on his verified Facebook account, Prime MinisterÔÇÖs Adviser on ExpatriatesÔÇÖ Welfare and Overseas Employment Mahdi Amin described the recognition as a ÔÇťhistoric momentÔÇŁ and a ÔÇťdemocratic milestoneÔÇŁ for Bangladesh.

He said the global magazine recognised Tarique RahmanÔÇÖs leadership, courage and contribution to what he described as the restoration of democracy on the international stage.

The adviser said in a difficult time when democracy was restricted, people did not have the right to vote and there was no freedom of speech, Tarique Rahman came forward with determination, courage and unwavering faith.

He instilled hope, strengthened the voice of the people, and spoke as the voice of the oppressed.

ÔÇťUnder his leadership, Bangladesh has returned to the path of democracy after overcoming many struggles. His role in establishing free and fair elections, freedom of expression, protection of human rights and the rule of law is recognised on the world stage today,ÔÇŁ he added.

Mahdi Amin said the Prime Minister is working relentlessly according to his plan he announced on his homecoming to build a non-discriminatory, inclusive and integrated Bangladesh.

The inclusion of the Prime Minister in this list by TIME is a strong recognition of his long fight for democracy, struggle and charismatic ability to lead the country, he said.

ÔÇťThis honour from TIME belongs to all of us. It belongs to every citizen of Bangladesh who believes that democracy is invincible and that the power of the people never gives up. This global recognition is the victory of democracy, the victory of the people, the victory of Bangladesh. Alhamdulillah,ÔÇŁ the adviser said.

item.title: Explainer: Why is Sudan at war, and what is the impact?
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The war in Sudan entered its fourth year on April 15 overshadowed by other regional and global turmoil, despite having created what the United Nations calls the world's worst humanitarian crisis.
The war pits the Sudanese army, led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, against the Rapid Support Forces, a ÔÇőparamilitary group led by General Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo.
The two men had joined forces in 2019 to overthrow veteran autocrat Omar al-Bashir, ÔÇîafter which Burhan became head of state. But having staged a coup that derailed a faltering transition towards civilian rule, they fell out over plans to launch a new transition and integrate their forces.
After war erupted on April 15, 2023, it quickly drew in myriad local militias fighting on either side, as well as foreign powers.
Most prominently, U.N. researchers, U.S. lawmakers, ÔÇőand the Sudanese army say the United Arab Emirates has provided critical support to the RSF via several of Sudan's neighbors , a charge the Gulf ÔÇőpower denies.
The Sudanese army counts other regional powers including Egypt , Turkey, Saudi Arabia , and Qatar as supporters to varying extents.
The RSF last year cemented control of the vast Darfur region, its traditional power base and the westernmost area of the country, and ÔÇőquickly began setting up a parallel government. The army maintains control over the eastern half of the country.
The fight is currently centered around the Kordofan region that lies ÔÇőin between, as well as a new front opened up by the RSF along the Sudanese border with Ethiopia in the southeast.
Drone warfare has displaced ground campaigns as the primary mode of fighting, and has allowed the RSF to overcome the army's previous air dominance. Like the shelling and bombardments that marked the war's earlier stages, it has also added to the human ÔÇőtoll of the conflict, claiming at least 700 civilian lives this year, according to the U.N.
The war has had a devastating impact ÔÇőon the Sudanese people, with the U.N. estimating that almost three-quarters are in need of humanitarian aid.
Famine or risk of famine has been declared in conflict hotspots across the country, ÔÇîoften exacerbated Ôüáby blockades and bureaucratic impediments placed by the warring parties.
Diseases including dengue fever have run rampant as the country's healthcare system collapsed in many areas.
Throughout the war, the RSF has carried out waves of ethnically targeted killings later cited as bearing the hallmarks of genocide by U.N. researchers , most recently in North Darfur's al-Fashir .
Violence and reduced funding have prevented the calculation of a comprehensive death toll. Sudan's health ministry tells Reuters it has documented 11,209 deaths across most of Sudan's states, but experts ÔÇősay excess deaths since the war began ÔÇőare in the hundreds of Ôüáthousands.
Despite overwhelming need, the U.N.'s 2026 appeal for aid to Sudan is only 17% funded, coming at a time when the U.S. has retreated from foreign aid, European donors make cuts, and Gulf powers focus on bilateral donations.
Aid agencies report ÔÇőscaling back their services, while Sudanese mutual aid groups, including the Nobel Peace Prize-nominated Emergency Response Rooms, have tried ÔÇőto fill the gap.
A Ôüástudy by aid agency Islamic Relief this week, however, showed that more than 40% of the local community kitchens run by these groups have had to shut down due to lack of support.
While the war has caught the attention of world leaders, including U.S. President Donald Trump, few attempts to Ôüáend it ÔÇőhave shown promise and competing regional interests have hindered efforts.
The U.S. has led a so-called ÔÇőQuad that includes Egypt, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates, which presented a preliminary proposal for a ceasefire to both sides last year.
As realities on the ground have changed, both the army ÔÇőand RSF have by turns welcomed and rejected mediation, with no let-up in fighting on the horizon.

item.title: Man goes on trial accused of attempting terrorist attack at London's Israeli embassy
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - A man armed with two knives and carrying a "martyrdom note" tried to carry out a terrorist attack at Israel's British embassy because he wanted to send a message about "the killing ÔÇőof children", prosecutors told a London court on Wednesday.
Abdullah Albadri, 34, who was born ÔÇîin Kuwait and twice entered Britain from France in a small boat, was arrested by two armed officers trying to scale the fence outside the embassy in west London last April, prosecutor Catherine Pattison said.
They found he was ÔÇőcarrying two knives and a piece of paper which appeared to be a note about ÔÇőmartyrdom, and later told police he wanted to send a message about stopping Ôüáthe war, a reference to the conflict between the Israelis and Palestinians, Pattison told London's Old ÔÇőBailey court.
"The prosecution say that Mr Albadri's intention was to use or threaten serious violence against the ÔÇőIsraeli government, to exact revenge for its alleged murder of children," she said.
"The existence and contents of his suspected martyrdom note, along with his two knives and material downloaded from his mobile phone, demonstrate his intention to use violence ÔÇőagainst people inside the Israeli embassy and sacrifice his own life in the process ÔÇô to die, ÔÇőin his words, 'for the glory of God'."
Albadri denies charges of preparing an act of terrorism and possession of two ÔÇîbladed Ôüáarticles, and he said in a statement to police that he had the knives because he was homeless.
Pattison said Albadri had first come to Britain on a small boat in August 2021 but about two years later, having withdrawn an application seeking permission to stay, he appeared to have left the ÔÇőcountry.
On April 12 last ÔÇőyear he again arrived Ôüáin Dover via a small boat, and began an application for asylum, saying he had left Kuwait for his and his family's safety because he ÔÇőwas a human rights activist.
However, exchanges on his phone showed his asylum ÔÇőclaim was Ôüárejected on April 24, after which his intention began to shift to "far more sinister matters" and he started searching for the location of the Israeli embassy, Pattison said.
On the afternoon of April 28, Albadri sent Ôüáa picture ÔÇőof a handwritten note alongside a knife to his mother.
Hours ÔÇőlater, Albadri was detained by the officers having almost succeeded in scaling the fence outside the embassy, Pattison said.
"'Why didn't you ÔÇőlet me in?'," Pattison said he told them.
The trial continues.

item.title: Kuwait detained US-Kuwaiti journalist over war posts, CPJ says
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Kuwaiti authorities have detained U.S.-Kuwaiti journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin who has not been seen for six weeks, a ÔÇőmedia watchdog said, during a wider crackdown by Gulf nations on people for filming or posting footage during the Iran ÔÇîwar .
Shihab-Eldin, a 41-year-old journalist who was visiting family in Kuwait, has not posted online or been seen in public since March 2, the New York-based Committee to Protect Journalists said on Monday.
CPJ said he had been charged with spreading false information, harming national security and misusing his mobile phone.
Kuwait enacted ÔÇőa law on March 15 including a provision carrying prison terms of up to 10 years for spreading false rumours ÔÇőrelated to military entities with the intent of undermining confidence in them.
Government spokespeople did not immediately respond Ôüáto Reuters' attempts to obtain comment on Shihab-Eldin's case via email and telephone.
Elsewhere in the Gulf, authorities have reported hundreds ÔÇőof arrests of people filming sites affected by the Middle East conflict or sharing related material online.
Iran responded to U.S.-Israeli strikes from February ÔÇő28 by targeting U.S. military and energy installations in neighbouring Gulf states.
Shihab-Eldin had posted before his detention on publicly available videos and images related to the war. The last posts on his Substack account include footage showing a U.S. fighter jet crash near a U.S. air base in Kuwait.
Shihab-Eldin describes himself ÔÇőas an independent journalist who has worked for various international media outlets including the New York Times, HBO, Vice, PBS, BBC ÔÇőand Al Jazeera. It was unclear if he was working, as well as seeing family, during his visit to Kuwait.
"We call on ÔÇîKuwait Ôüáto release Ahmed Shihab-Eldin and drop all charges against him,ÔÇŁ said CPJ Regional Director Sara Qudah. ÔÇťJournalism is not a crime."
U.N. official Francesca Albanese, who is the special rapporteur for the Palestinian territories, also called for his release, praising him as a "journalist of rare strength, integrity and compassion."
Reuters could not independently verify the circumstances of his detention or the reported charges.
In Kuwait, the Interior Ministry said on February ÔÇő28 that the public should refrain ÔÇőfrom filming missile interceptions, sharing Ôüásuch footage on social media, or filming security personnel at work.
It urged residents to rely on official sources.
On March 2, state news agency KUNA reported that the ministry had arrested a number of ÔÇőpeople over what it described as violations affecting public security, including filming events and circulating video ÔÇőclips.
Reuters could Ôüánot independently confirm the arrests.
Qatar's Interior Ministry said on March 9 that 313 people of various nationalities had been arrested for filming and circulating video clips, spreading misleading information and rumours, and publishing material aimed at stirring public concern.
In the United Arab Emirates, Abu Dhabi police in Ôüáa statement ÔÇőon April 8 said 375 people of various nationalities had been arrested over ÔÇőfilming locations and sharing misleading information on social media in connection with "current events", and that the cases had been referred to prosecutors.
Reuters could not determine how the ÔÇőcases have proceeded.
There was no immediate response from Qatar or the UAE to Reuters requests for comments.

item.title: UN human rights office raises concern around trial of Chinese dissident artist
item.description:
GENEVA, April 15 (Reuters) - The United Nations human rights office on Wednesday said it was concerned by the ÔÇőtrial of Chinese dissident artist Gao Zhen, famous ÔÇîfor making provocative satirical sculptures of former leader Mao Zedong.
Gao was tried at the end of March over accusations of "slandering national heroes and ÔÇőmartyrs", the U.N. human rights office said ÔÇö a law ÔÇőwhich it said came into force more than Ôüáa decade after the satirical sculptures were created.
"The case against ÔÇőhim raises concerns with regard to retroactive application of criminal ÔÇőlaw and use of criminal sanctions to punish artistic expression, undermining the principle of legality," the office said in a statement.
The Chinese mission ÔÇőin Geneva did not immediately respond to a Reuters ÔÇőrequest for comment.
Gao, who was detained in 2024 during a visit from ÔÇîthe ÔüáU.S., faces a maximum three-year prison sentence, said his wife Zhao Yaliang and Shane Yi, a researcher at the Chinese Human Rights Defenders group which operates outside the country.
The ÔÇőU.N. rights office ÔÇőcalled for ÔüáGao's immediate release from detention, where he is being held pending judgment after his closed-door ÔÇőtrial concluded on March 30, citing concerns ÔÇőthat his Ôüáhealth is deteriorating.
The one-day trial took place at Sanhe City People's Court in Hebei province, neighbouring the capital Beijing, and Ôüáended ÔÇőwithout a verdict, Zhao and Yi ÔÇőtold Reuters, citing information from his lawyers.
Verdicts are often announced months later in ÔÇősuch trials.

item.title: Spain's Senate blackout probe blames grid operator, government, watchdog
item.description:
MADRID, April 15 (Reuters) - An inquiry by the Spanish Senate blames the government, as well the country's grid operator and energy ÔÇőwatchdog, for last year's unprecedented blackout, according to ÔÇîpreliminary conclusions made public on Wednesday.
The probe by the upper house - which is controlled by the opposition People's Party (PP) - is the first ÔÇőto assign blame for the outage that plunged large ÔÇőparts of Spain and Portugal into darkness for Ôüáup to 16 hours on April 28, 2025.
The nineÔÇĹmonth ÔÇőinvestigation said the blackout was not an unforeseeable accident, but ÔÇőrather caused by longÔÇĹstanding structural weaknesses that were already known.
"The blackout was the result of a known vulnerability, of a system that had ÔÇőbeen sending warnings for some time,
and of a failure ÔÇőto act with the required diligence," PP senator Alicia Garcia told reporters.
The ÔÇîpreliminary Ôüáreport cited repeated voltage swings in the weeks and months leading up to the blackout as evidence of mounting problems in the electricity system.
The Senate commission held grid operator ÔÇőRed Electrica, a ÔÇőunit of ÔüáRedeia (REDE.MC) , opens new tab , and the Energy Ministry primarily responsible for the outage, while also criticising energy and ÔÇőantitrust regulator CNMC for what it described as ÔÇőregulatory Ôüáand supervisory inaction.
The inquiry heard testimonies from dozens of witnesses, including Energy Minister Sara Aagesen, Redeia chair Beatriz Corredor and ÔüáCNMC ÔÇőhead Cani Fernandez.
The final report is ÔÇődue later this week, though no changes to the preliminary conclusions are expected.

item.title: Counter-terrorism police investigate attempted arson attack on London synagogue
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - British police, including specialist counter-terrorism officers, have launched an investigation after an attempted arson attack on a synagogue in north ÔÇőLondon, in the latest incident involving the capital's Jewish community.
Two ÔÇîsuspects wearing dark clothing and balaclavas threw two bottles, suspected of containing petrol, and a brick at the synagogue in Finchley shortly after midnight on Wednesday, ÔÇőpolice said. Neither bottle ignited and there was no damage ÔÇőor any injuries.
However, the incident, which is being treated Ôüáas an antisemitic hate crime, comes just weeks after four ambulances ÔÇőbelonging to the Jewish volunteer emergency service Hatzola were torched while they were ÔÇőparked near a synagogue in the Golders Green area of north London.
"We are aware of the significant concern that this incident will cause in the community, ÔÇőparticularly in the wake of the arson attack in Golders Green ÔÇőlast month," said Detective Chief Superintendent Luke Williams.
Local member of parliament Sarah Sackman said ÔÇîshe Ôüárefused to accept such incidents becoming "the new normal".
"This shocking attempt to harm a local synagogue follows a series of alarming attacks on the Jewish community in Finchley and Golders Green," she said in a ÔÇőstatement.
"British Jews must ÔÇőbe free to Ôüágo about their lives without fear - whether taking their children to nursery or attending synagogue. We do ÔÇőnot want to live behind ever higher walls."
Attacks on Jewish ÔÇőpeople and ÔüáJewish targets have risen in Britain since the October 2023 Hamas assault on Israel that triggered the war in Gaza, according to police Ôüáand community ÔÇőgroups.
In February, the government announced about ÔÇő28 million pounds ($38 million) would be provided to help provide security at synagogues, Jewish schools ÔÇőand community centres.
($1 = 0.7369 pounds)

item.title: Russian LNG under US sanctions heads for the first time to India
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - A cargo ÔÇőof liquefied natural gas from Russia's Portovaya plant on the Baltic Sea that ÔÇîis under U.S. sanctions is on its way to India, LSEG shipping data showed on Wednesday.
If it reaches its destination, it would be the first such delivery to India since U.S. President Donald Trump last year said ÔÇőPrime Minister Narendra Modi had assured him India would stop buying Russian energy.
India has ÔÇőnever publicly confirmed any such pledge and has said its buying decisions are Ôüáguided by price, supply security and consumer interests. As one of the world's biggest energy ÔÇőimporters, it is now greatly exposed to the disruption and price rises caused by the Middle Eastern war ÔÇőand the resulting closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
The small-scale Portovaya plant, which has a production capacity of 1.5 million tons of LNG per year, started operations in September 2022.
Exports from the plant have been interrupted by ÔÇőadditional sanctions over the Ukraine war that were imposed in January 2025 to disrupt Russia's ability ÔÇőto produce and export LNG and reduce its revenue from the trade.
So far Russia ÔÇîhas Ôüásupplied LNG under sanctions to China's port of Beihai from Portovaya and also from its Arctic LNG 2 plant.
Earlier on Wednesday, Russian energy giant Gazprom (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab delivered a second post-sanctions LNG cargo from Portovaya plant to China, LSEG data showed.
If India is buying Russian LNG, that would provide another market as ÔÇőRussian President Vladimir Putin seeks ÔÇőto divert LNG Ôüáfrom Europe before the European Union enforces its import ban on Russia's LNG from 2027.
Kunpeng, a 138,200 cubic metre tanker, is heading to Dahej ÔÇőLNG import terminal in western India, according to LSEG data.
"The cargo, if ÔÇődelivered, would Ôüáopen up a second market for U.S.-sanctioned Russian LNG, with all previous deliveries from sanctioned Russian terminals delivering to ChinaÔÇÖs Beihai," said Martin Senior, head of LNG pricing at industry publication Argus.
Before the sanctions, ÔüáPortovaya shipped ÔÇőtwo cargoes a month on average during winter. Since ÔÇőMarch 2025, apart from the two shipments to China, it has been sending one cargo per month to the Russian ÔÇőwestern exclave of Kaliningrad.

item.title: Eni's Vaar Energi plans to boost oil output from Arctic Goliat field
item.description:
OSLO, April 15 (Reuters) - Norway's Vaar Energi (VAR.OL) , opens new tab , a subsidiary of Italy's Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab , said on Wednesday it plans to ÔÇőboost crude production from its Arctic Goliat ÔÇîoilfield along with minority stakeholder Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab .
The companies will invest around $360 million in the project, which will increase Goliat's ÔÇőoil output from 2029 onwards and extend the ÔÇőfield's production lifetime by approximately 10 years Ôüáto around 2050, Vaar said in a statement.
The ÔÇőproject will establish a pipeline connection to the ÔÇőnearby Snoehvit gas facility, allowing the removal of large amounts of natural gas from Goliat that currently restricts the flow ÔÇőof oil.
"The incremental oil will be sold ÔÇőfrom the start, while the gas will be exported to ÔÇîthe ÔüáHammerfest LNG plant under a gas bank arrangement, agreed with Snoehvit, and sold when processing capacity is available," Eni said.
The Goliat field produced more gas ÔÇőthan oil ÔÇőlast year Ôüábut has no ability to export the gas, forcing the operator to reinject ÔÇőmost of it into the reservoir, ÔÇőthe company Ôüáhas said.
Goliat's average daily oil production stood at just over 22,000 barrels per day in 2025, data Ôüáfrom ÔÇőthe Norwegian Offshore Directorate shows.
Vaar owns ÔÇő65% of the Goliat licence while Equinor owns 35%.

item.title: Japan refiners run at two-thirds capacity, awaiting crude from outside the Gulf
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japanese refineries ran at two-thirds of their capacity last week, little changed from the ÔÇîprevious week, highlighting the short-term challenges of replacing their Middle Eastern crude supply, cut by the Iran war.
Refinery runs stood at 67.8% of the designed capacity for the week to April 11, in line with 67.7% in the previous week and well below the ÔÇőlevels of more than 80% seen before the start of the war in late February, Petroleum Association ÔÇőof Japan data showed on Wednesday.
The outlook for next month may be better, as the Ôüácountry has released oil from its national stockpile and managed to secure substitutes for over half the volume it ÔÇőimported last May via the Strait of Hormuz, officials have said. Overall, Middle Eastern crudes normally make up 95% ÔÇőof Japan's imports.
However, Japanese refineries have limitations on the volume of other crudes they can run, as their plants are designed to process mainly medium-sour Middle Eastern crude.
"Japan could likely push non-Middle East crude to around 30-50% of its slate in the short ÔÇőterm. But fully replacing Middle Eastern supply is difficult," said Nithin Prakash, an analyst with Rystad Energy.
The new ÔÇősupply is largely coming from the U.S., Japan's closest ally. Tokyo has also contacted producers in countries including Malaysia, Azerbaijan, Brazil, ÔÇîNigeria and ÔüáAngola.
The refiners can mix the Middle Eastern crudes with light-sweet crude from the U.S. and West Africa along with some medium grades from the Caspian region and parts of Latin America, Prakash said, adding that the shift would increase yields of gasoline and naphtha and reduce diesel and jet output.
PAJ has suspended publication of Japan's petroleum product stocks, ÔÇőciting the change in ÔÇősupply structure.
Idemitsu Kosan (5019.T) , opens new tab said it Ôüáis exploring crude purchases from various countries, including in North and South America, adding there are challenges related to refinery equipment when processing non-Middle Eastern oils.
Taiyo Oil said it ÔÇőcan process more than 50% of non-Middle Eastern grades, including those from North ÔÇőAmerica, Southeast Asia Ôüáand grades from Oceania.
Japan's top refiner Eneos (5020.T) , opens new tab and Cosmo Energy (5021.T) , opens new tab declined to comment on their refinery specifications.
Japanese refiners have not made major upgrades at their plants, built mainly during the industrial boom of the 1960s to 1970s, or invested Ôüáin new ÔÇőones, as fuel demand is declining.
Gasoline demand in Japan is forecast ÔÇőto decline 10% over the five years to 2030, according to an April 7 document from the industry ministry, and all major refining groups ÔÇőhave been expanding into renewables in the recent years.

item.title: Exclusive: Iran offers proposal allowing ships to exit Oman side of Hormuz free of attack, source says
item.description:
DUBAI, April 15 (Reuters) - Iran could consider allowing ships to sail freely through the Oman side ÔÇőof the Strait of Hormuz without risk of attack ÔÇîas part of proposals it has offered in negotiations with the United States if a deal is clinched to prevent renewed conflict, a ÔÇősource briefed by Tehran said.
The war has resulted in ÔÇőthe largest-ever disruption of global oil and gas supplies Ôüádue to Iran's interruption of traffic through the strait, ÔÇőwhich handles about 20% of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas ÔÇőflows.
Hundreds of tankers and other ships and 20,000 seafarers have been stuck inside the Gulf since the Iran war began on February 28.
The source, ÔÇőwho declined to be identified due to the sensitivity of ÔÇőthe matter, said Iran could be willing to let ships use the ÔÇîother Ôüáside of the narrow strait in Omani waters without any hindrance from Tehran.
The source did not say whether Iran would also agree to clear any mines it may have placed ÔÇőin that stretch ÔÇőof water or Ôüáif all ships - even those linked to Israel - would be allowed to pass freely.
But the ÔÇősource added that the proposal hinged on whether ÔÇőWashington Ôüáwas prepared to meet Tehran's demands, a condition that was central to any potential breakthrough with the Strait of Hormuz.
The White ÔüáHouse ÔÇődid not immediately respond to a ÔÇőrequest for comment.

item.title: Kremlin says US has rejected its proposal that Russia take Iranian uranium stocks
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The Kremlin's spokesman was quoted as saying on Wednesday that the United States had rejected its ÔÇőproposal that Russia take all of Iran's ÔÇîenriched uranium out of the country as a way to help resolve the Middle East conflict.
Russia first proposed last June that ÔÇőit take control of Iran's uranium stock, ÔÇőbut no action was taken. According to news Ôüáreports, Russia issued the proposal again this week.
"Russia ÔÇőwas prepared to accept Iran's enriched uranium on its ÔÇőterritory," state news agency RIA said Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told the Indian television channel India Today.
"This would be a good ÔÇődecision. But unfortunately the American side rejected this ÔÇőproposal."
U.S. news reports have quoted sources as saying that the ÔÇîadministration Ôüáof U.S. President Donald Trump had ruled out the proposal. For its part, Iran had said any decision would depend on whether it is able to ÔÇőreach an agreement ÔÇőwith the ÔüáU.S., including on its nuclear programme.
The U.S. has cited Iran's stockpile of enriched ÔÇőuranium -- and the possibility it might be ÔÇőable Ôüáto secure a nuclear weapon -- as grounds for its attacks on Iran.
A Russian deputy foreign minister last year Ôüásuggested ÔÇőRussia was willing to remove ÔÇőthe stockpile from Iran and convert it to civilian reactor fuel to ÔÇőhelp facilitate negotiations.

item.title: El Salvador to apply life sentences to minors for serious crimes
item.description:
SAN SALVADOR, April 15 (Reuters) - Salvadoran President Nayib Bukele has enacted legal reforms allowing ÔÇőlife imprisonment for minors as young ÔÇîas 12 convicted of homicide, terrorism, or rape, according to the country's official gazette.
The measures, published ÔÇőon Tuesday and scheduled to take ÔÇőeffect April 26, follow a constitutional amendment Ôüáapproved in March by the government-controlled Congress.
The ÔÇőchanges eliminate special legal procedures previously available ÔÇőto offenders aged 12 to 18, though they include provisions for periodic sentence reviews and potential supervised ÔÇőrelease.
The constitutional amendment came days after an ÔÇőinternational legal panel reported " reasonable grounds " to suggest El Salvador ÔÇîhas Ôüácommitted crimes against humanity during a years-long state of emergency.
The United Nations human rights office criticized the reforms for violating children's ÔÇőrights. Bukele ÔÇődefended the Ôüámeasures, saying the previous legal framework granted impunity to young criminals.
The ÔÇő44-year-old president's ongoing state of emergency, ÔÇőwhich Ôüásuspends various constitutional guarantees, has led to the detention of more than 90,000 people. ÔüáHumanitarian ÔÇőorganizations estimate at least 500 ÔÇőof those detainees have died in state custody.

item.title: At least 16 reported dead from heavy rains in Hispaniola
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Days of torrential rainfall over northwest Haiti and neighboring Dominican Republic have killed ÔÇősome 16 people, according to civil ÔÇîprotection and local media reports on Wednesday.
At least 12 people were killed in northern Haiti as floods ÔÇőhit communities in Port-de-Paix, Saint-Louis du ÔÇőNord and Anse-a-Foleur, local newspaper Le Nouvelliste Ôüáreported.
In the Dominican Republic, which shares ÔÇőthe Caribbean island of Hispaniola with Haiti, local ÔÇőoutlet Listin Diario reported that four people had died, some swept away by swollen rivers, while an ÔÇőinfant girl was killed in her home ÔÇőwhen a wall collapsed on her.
Dominican authorities said on ÔÇîMonday Ôüáthat more than 30,000 people were forced to evacuate their homes, and that the rains were expected to strengthen again this weekend.
In ÔÇőthe nearby U.S. ÔÇőterritory Ôüáof Puerto Rico, authorities urged residents to avoid flooded highways as ÔÇőrains are expected to continue into ÔÇőthe Ôüáafternoon.
Just days earlier, a stampede at northern Haiti's Laferriere Citadel, a UNESCO World Heritage site, killed Ôüá25 ÔÇőpeople after people began forcing ÔÇőtheir way onto the site during an annual event ÔÇőas it started to rain.

item.title: Netherlands to spend nearly $300 million euros on drones for Ukraine
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, April 15 (Reuters) - The Netherlands will spend 248 million euros ($293 million) ÔÇőon producing drones for Ukraine , Dutch ÔÇîDefence Minister Dilan Yesilgoz-Zegerius said on Wednesday.
"Drones play a crucial role ÔÇőon the modern battlefield. ÔÇőUkrainians deploy them with incredible Ôüáskill to repel the incessant ÔÇőRussian attacks," she said after ÔÇőmeeting her counterparts from NATO countries and the alliance's Secretary General Mark Rutte ÔÇőin Berlin on Wednesday.
"Thanks to ÔÇőthe good cooperation with Ukraine, we are ÔÇîlearning Ôüádirectly from this. This also offers opportunities for our business community," she added. The ÔÇődrones will ÔÇőbe Ôüámanufactured in the Netherlands and Ukraine.
NATO allies have ÔÇőalready provided over $4.5 billion ÔÇőin Ôüámilitary equipment for Ukraine, according to data from the U.S. Ôüáambassador ÔÇőto NATO Matthew ÔÇőWhitaker released in February.
($1 = 0.8475 euros)

item.title: Europe sees record jet inflows from US in April
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Europe was seeing record inflows of jet fuel from the United States, data from Kpler and LSEG showed, as the region attempts to shore up fuel supplies to make up for the disruption ÔÇőof imports from the Middle East.
The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz due ÔÇőto the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran has blocked flows from the Middle East, Ôüáon which Europe depends on for nearly 75% of its jet fuel imports, or around 375,000 ÔÇőbarrels per day. This has led European airlines to urge the European Union to step in ÔÇőwith emergency measures, including widespread airspace closures, as per a document seen by Reuters.
Inflows from the U.S. are due to reach between 149,000 to 200,000 barrels per day so far in April, based on vessels discharged and those still ÔÇődue to, which is already at record highs per data going as far back as 2015 ÔÇőon LSEG and 2017 on Kpler.
Despite the requirement in the EU countries to maintain 90 days of emergency ÔÇîoil reserves, Ôüáthis doesn't stipulate how much of it should be a specific fuel. Per the International Energy Agency , Spain is a net exporter of jet fuel, while Britain, which is also the regionÔÇÖs largest consumer, imports 65% of its demand. Stocks of jet fuel held independently in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp refining and ÔÇőstorage hub stood at ÔÇőtheir lowest since March Ôüá2023 last week.
The U.S. is, however, already exporting at record highs - in the week ending April 3, the country exported an estimated 442,000 barrels of ÔÇőjet fuel in total, double the 219,000 barrels averaged last year per ÔÇődata from Ôüáthe Energy Information Administration.
While the U.S. is the top consumer of jet fuel globally, exports to regions with a worse fuel crunch like Europe and Asia were fetching better prices.
The IEA also added in its Ôüámonthly ÔÇőreport that if European markets are unable to secure more ÔÇőthan 50% of volumes lost from the Middle East, then stocks would hit the crucial 23-day stockpile level in June - ÔÇőthe level at which physical shortages would begin.

item.title: US Treasury chief says he expects gasoline to be in $3 dollar range this summer
item.description: WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Treasury Secretary ÔÇőScott Bessent ÔÇîon Wednesday told ÔÇőreporters ÔÇőhe has been meeting Ôüáwith ÔÇőMiddle East ÔÇőcounterparts about the Iran oil ÔÇőcrisis ÔÇőand that he ÔÇîexpects ÔüáU.S. consumers will see gasoline ÔÇőprices ÔÇődrop Ôüáto the $3 ÔÇőrange this ÔÇősummer.

item.title: Russia delivers second post-sanctions LNG cargo from Portovaya to China, data shows
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russian energy giant Gazprom (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab has delivered a second post-sanctions LNG cargo from the Baltic ÔÇőSea's Portovaya plant to China, LSEG data ÔÇîshowed on Wednesday.
The small-scale Portovaya plant, which has a production capacity of 1.5 million tons of LNG per ÔÇőyear, started operations in September 2022.
Exports from the ÔÇőplant have been interrupted by additional U.S. sanctions , opens new tab over Ôüáthe Ukraine war that were imposed in January ÔÇő2025 to disrupt Russia's ability to produce and ÔÇőexport LNG and reduce its revenue from the trade.
Gas carrier Valera, formerly known as Velikiy Novgorod, loaded the cargo at the ÔÇőBaltic Sea plant on January 25 and ÔÇődelivered it to the Beihai LNG terminal on Wednesday, LSEG ÔÇîship-tracking Ôüádata showed.
The first cargo from the plant after a hiatus was delivered to the terminal last December.
Another of Russia's sanctioned plants, Arctic LNG-2, also supplies LNG to Beihai.
Before ÔÇőthe sanctions, ÔÇőPortovaya shipped Ôüátwo cargoes a month on average during winter. Since March 2025, beyond the ÔÇőtwo shipments to China, it has ÔÇőbeen sending Ôüáone cargo per month to the Russian western exclave of Kaliningrad.
In the early stages of its Ôüáoperations, ÔÇőmost cargoes from Portovaya were delivered ÔÇőto Turkey and Greece with markets subsequently widening to China, ÔÇőSpain and Italy.

item.title: Earthquake of magnitude 5.93 strikes off coast of Costa Rica, GFZ says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - An earthquake ÔÇőof magnitude ÔÇî5.93 struck off ÔÇőthe coast ÔÇőof Costa Rica Ôüáearly ÔÇőon Wednesday, ÔÇőthe German Research Centre ÔÇőfor ÔÇőGeosciences said.
The quake ÔÇîhit Ôüáat 0656 GMT at a ÔÇődepth ÔÇőof Ôüá18 km (11.18 ÔÇőmiles), ÔÇőGFZ Ôüásaid.

item.title: Israeli military continues to strike Hezbollah amid Lebanon peace talks, Netanyahu says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, April 15 (Reuters) - Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said on Wednesday the Israeli military continued to strike at Hezbollah and ÔÇőwas about to overcome what he described as the militia's ÔÇîstronghold of Bint Jbeil, as pressure mounted for a ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon.
In a video statement, Netanyahu said he has instructed the military ÔÇőto continue reinforcing the security zone in southern Lebanon ÔÇőwhile at the same time negotiating a peace Ôüádeal with Beirut.
"These negotiations have not taken place for over ÔÇő40 years. They are happening now because we are very ÔÇőstrong, and countries are coming to us - not only Lebanon," he said.
He said that in talks with Lebanon, Israel has two main objectives - dismantling ÔÇőof Hezbollah and a sustainable peace that is "achieved through ÔÇőstrength."
Conflict between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon reignited after U.S.-Israeli strikes ÔÇîon ÔüáIran on February 28 triggered a wider conflict in the Middle East.
Netanyahu said Israeli forces were focused on Bint Jbeil, which he called the capital of Hezbollah in southern Lebanon.
"We are, in ÔÇőeffect, about ÔÇőto eliminate this Ôüágreat stronghold of Hezbollah," he said.
On Iran, Netanyahu said the U.S. keeps Israel updated and ÔÇőthe two countries are aligned on their goals ÔÇőto Ôüásee enriched nuclear material removed from Iran, the cancellation of enrichment capabilities within Iran, and the opening of the Strait of Hormuz.
"It is too Ôüáearly ÔÇőto say how this matter will ÔÇőend, or even how it will progress," he said. Should fighting resume, "we are ÔÇőprepared for any scenario."

item.title: White House denies U.S. requested ceasefire, says new talks may happen in Pakistan
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Reports that the White House has requested a ceasefire in the Iran ÔÇőwar are wrong, press secretary Karoline Leavitt told ÔÇîreporters on Wednesday but added that discussions about a second round of talks with the Iranians were ongoing and productive.
Speaking at a White ÔÇőHouse press briefing, Leavitt said any fresh talks ÔÇőwould likely be in Pakistan again as it Ôüáhas emerged as the "only mediator" in the effort to ÔÇőend the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
"These conversations are productive and ÔÇőongoing, and that's where we are right now. I've also seen some reporting about the potentiality for in-person discussions. Again, those discussions ÔÇőare being had, but nothing is official until you ÔÇőhear it from us here at the White House, but we feel ÔÇîgood Ôüáabout the prospects of a deal," Leavitt said.
The talks last weekend broke down without an agreement to end the war, which President Donald Trump began alongside Israel on ÔÇőFebruary 28, triggering ÔÇőIranian attacks Ôüáon Iran's Gulf neighbors and reigniting a conflict between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah in ÔÇőLebanon.
The war has led Iran to effectively shut ÔÇőthe ÔüáStrait of Hormuz - a vital artery for global crude and gas shipments - to ships other than its own, sharply reducing Ôüáexports ÔÇőfrom the Gulf, particularly to Asia ÔÇőand Europe, and leaving energy importers scrambling for alternative supplies.

item.title: US Senate Republicans block bid to rein in Trump Iran war powers, voting continues
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - A majority of ÔÇőthe U.S. ÔÇîSenate backed President Donald Trump's ÔÇőmilitary campaign ÔÇőagainst Iran on Wednesday, Ôüávoting to ÔÇőblock a ÔÇőDemocratic-led resolution aiming to stop the ÔÇőwar until ÔÇőhostilities are authorized by ÔÇîCongress.
As Ôüávoting continued, the tally was 51-47 to ÔÇőblock ÔÇőthe Ôüáwar powers resolution, as ÔÇőalmost ÔÇőall Ôüáof Trump's fellow Republicans voted Ôüáagainst ÔÇőit.

item.title: Israeli attacks prevent Lebanese from burying their dead in ancestral lands
item.description:
BEIRUT April 15 - Brothers Ali and Wael ÔÇőSabbagh stood facing the remains of the building that contained their family home for more than 50 years.
The ÔÇîsite was pounded into rubble by Israeli strikes last week in an attack that killed their mother, brother, and the family's helper.
Their grief has been compounded by their inability to properly bury their mother and brother ÔÇö a plight shared by other families from south Lebanon. Unable to head to their ancestral lands to ÔÇőhonor their dead, they are forced to bury them temporarily in Beirut until it becomes safe enough to allow their ÔÇőremains to be moved to their final resting place.
"We come from the south of Lebanon, from Nabatieh, and Ôüáthat is being bombarded on a daily basis," Wael said. "We want them to be buried in the family cemetery, but unfortunately we're ÔÇőunable to do that."
Israel has vowed to establish a buffer zone in southern Lebanon and maintain control over territory ÔÇőup to about 30 km (20 miles) north of Israel's border.
Nabatieh, a major city in the south, has been mostly reduced to rubble. The brothers said they cannot risk the journey south, nor can they send someone in their place for fear of them being killed in the strikes.
"Nobody should have to go ÔÇőthrough this. And unfortunately, my brother, sister, and I are not the only ones going through this," Wael said. "Hundreds, if not thousands, of ÔÇőfamilies...are mourning in the same way I am, not understanding what happened."
The Israeli attacks on Beirut last week were some of the most violent strikes on ÔÇîthe Lebanese Ôüácapital in decades. Across the country, more than 350 people were killed, said Lebanese authorities.
More than 2,160 people have been killed in Lebanon by Israeli attacks since the war between Hezbollah and Israel erupted last month. Hezbollah had lobbed rockets at Israel, prompting Israel to retaliate and launch an all-out war on Lebanon. More than 1.2 million people have been displaced in Lebanon since, said Lebanese authorities.
The Israeli military has said its ÔÇőoperations in Lebanon target Hezbollah.
Ali and ÔÇőWael's mother's body was found Ôüáin her home. It took two days for the family to track down their brother, whom they were able to identify by his arm tattoo. The family made arrangements to repatriate the body of ÔÇőthe house helper to Ethiopia.
The brothers spoke to Reuters a day after Lebanese and Israeli officials ÔÇőmet in Washington in Ôüáa rare encounter between representatives of governments that have technically been in a state of war since Israel was established in 1948.
"We're always going to be under the foot of the Israelis," Wael said. The brothers said they will try to seek legal action against the people behind the Ôüákilling of ÔÇőtheir family members.
Ali lamented the cycle of death, destruction and resurgence that has ÔÇőmarked the lives of the Lebanese for most of their country's existence.
Already, behind the Sabbaghs' bombed-out home, construction workers were busy working on a new building.
"Come back in a few years, ÔÇőthere's a new building, new kids, new mothers, new brothers," Ali said. "I will come. I will remember. Nobody else will."

item.title: Russia warns drone plans for Ukraine are pulling Europe deeper into war
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia's defence ministry warned on Wednesday that European ÔÇőplans to step up drone supplies to Ukraine are dragging ÔÇîthose countries deeper into a war with Russia.
The ministry said it believes governments in a number of EU countries have decided to increase ÔÇőthe production and supply of drones to Ukraine, a ÔÇőmove Moscow views as a step that is escalating Ôüáthe conflict.
It published a list of factories and enterprises in ÔÇőseveral European countries it alleges manufacture drones or drone components, ÔÇőand gave their addresses, including sites in Britain, Germany, Spain, Italy, Israel and Poland, among others.
"The European public should not only have a clear ÔÇőunderstanding of the true causes of the threats to their ÔÇősecurity, but also be aware of the addresses and locations of Ukrainian and ÔÇîjoint Ôüáenterprises producing UAVs and components for Ukraine within their own countries," it said.
Former Russian President Dmitry Medvedev, now deputy chair of Russia's Security Council, said in a subsequent post on X ÔÇőthat the list ÔÇőpublished by Ôüáthe military amounted to a list of potential targets for Russia's armed forces.
"When strikes become a ÔÇőreality depends on what comes next. Sleep well, ÔÇőEuropean Ôüápartners!" he said.
Russian officials, including Medvedev, have repeatedly made public statements that have been perceived as threats to European countries over their Ôüásupport for ÔÇőUkraine. However, these have generally taken ÔÇőthe form of warnings and veiled hints rather than outright announcements of imminent ÔÇőstrikes.

item.title: Colombia court orders Petro government to return emergency funds
item.description:
BOGOTA, April 15 (Reuters) - Colombia's Constitutional Court ordered President Gustavo Petro's government on Wednesday to return funds collected during an ÔÇőeconomic emergency that was declared unconstitutional.
The decision is the latest blow ÔÇîto the Petro administration's finances, strained by setbacks in Congress, where several proposals for fiscal reforms have failed to gain support.
The court had struck down the economic emergency declared ÔÇőby Petro's government in January to raise 11 trillion pesos ($3.07 billion) ÔÇőto finance part of this year's budget.
The government had collected 1.67 Ôüátrillion pesos ($467 million) as a result of the decrees enacted during the ÔÇőeconomic emergency, according to a source at the country's National Tax and Customs ÔÇőDirectorate who spoke on condition of anonymity.
The economic emergency is a state of exception that allows the government to make decisions by decree without prior authorization from Congress.
Among the measures ÔÇőthe government had established during the state of emergency were a tax ÔÇőon liquid assets exceeding 2.1 billion pesos, a special income tax for the financial sector, ÔÇîand Ôüáa 19% tax on the sale of alcoholic beverages and gambling, as well as on certain vehicles, motorcycles, and aircraft, among others.
On previous occasions when the court declared other economic emergencies unconstitutional, the ruling was not retroactive; meaning it ÔÇődid not require the ÔÇőgovernment to return Ôüáthe funds already collected.
Latin AmericaÔÇÖs fourth-largest economy is experiencing a deterioration in its finances, which forced the government in ÔÇőJune to suspend a fiscal ruling under which the Ministry ÔÇőof ÔüáFinance raised the fiscal deficit target for 2025 to 7.1% of GDP, from an original 5.1%.
Last week, Finance Minister German Avila announced that he would present a new Ôüátax reform ÔÇőbill to Congress worth 16 trillion pesos ($4.47 ÔÇőbillion) to finance this yearÔÇÖs spending budget, less than three months before the end of the legislative ÔÇőperiod.
($1= 3,578.82 pesos)

item.title: Sovereign borrowers launch platform to amplify voice in debt talks
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - A group of officials from developing economies launched on Wednesday ÔÇîthe Borrowers' Platform, an effort backed by the United Nations to give debt-hit nations a stronger collective voice in dealings with creditors.
United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said at the launch event that the platform is essential for power relations to change.
"Today, ÔÇőwe launch a breakthrough in global financing, a platform in which borrowing countries sit together, ÔÇőlearn from each other and speak with a collective voice," Guterres said.
He said 3.4 Ôüábillion people live in countries that spend more on debt service than on health or education.
The ÔÇőinitiative is seen partly as a counter for institutions like the Paris Club, where official creditors meet to ÔÇődiscuss negotiation strategies when a common debtor encounters problems with repayment.
This platform aims also to be an experience repository of sorts, where members share their knowledge and new governments, or new countries, avoid arriving at the table with a ÔÇőlack of preparedness.
"What was once a long-standing aspiration of developing countries has now become a concrete and a ÔÇőcollective step forward," said Ahmed Kouchouk, Egypt's finance minister. "Today stands as a strong statement of intent that the voice of borrowing ÔÇînations Ôüáand countries belongs at the very center of the global financial dialogue."
The event, presided over by Egypt as chair of the working group, is due to formally open an interim phase for the platform, establish interim leadership and adopt a work program running through October 2026, according to the launch agenda.
The ÔÇőworking group that developed the ÔÇődraft modalities was Ôüámade up of Egypt, Colombia, Honduras, Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan and Zambia. Pakistan served as vice chair.
Under the draft framework, full membership would be voluntary and limited ÔÇőto developing countries that are UN member states, net borrowers and not ÔÇőfull members of Ôüácreditor groupings. The proposed structure includes a Governing Council of finance ministers, central bank governors or equivalent officials, and a Steering Committee of senior technical officials.
"The launch of the Borrowers' Platform is a major milestone Ôüáin rebalancing ÔÇőpower inequalities in global economic governance," said Iolanda Fresnillo, policy and ÔÇőadvocacy manager at the European Network on Debt and Development (Eurodad), in a statement.
"This long-overdue initiative is a first step towards breaking creditor domination over decision-making ÔÇőon sovereign debt issues."

item.title: Allbirds lacks footing for dramatic AI pivot
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - After failing to sustain its eco-friendly sneaker empire, Allbirds (BIRD.O) , opens new tab has an even woolier proposition. The company, once valued at ÔÇőmore than $4 billion, sold , opens new tab what remains of its footwear business last month for just $39 million, and is ÔÇînow racing into , opens new tab the world of artificial intelligence. These sorts of bizarre pivots work occasionally, but the odds are long.
Some modern corporate giants have sprouted from highly unusual seeds. South Korean smartphone and chip maker Samsung once exported dried fish while Nokia made the unlikely ÔÇőswitch from rubber-boot manufacturing to wireless telecom. India's Wipro started by selling cooking oil before becoming an ÔÇőIT powerhouse. Shell is so named because of its origins as a collectibles shop where Ôüáfounder Marcus Samuel once sold , opens new tab decorative sea-creature coverings before it later commissioned the first oil tanker to traverse the ÔÇőSuez Canal.
Most such ambitious metamorphoses flop, however. They suffer from a lack of management skill, capital, luck, competition and myriad ÔÇőother factors. After all, defeat is the eventual fate for most ventures, and the bigger the gambit the lower the chance for success.
Jumping onto the latest bandwagon, as Allbirds is doing, also tends to be little more than a gimmick, or worse. The Long ÔÇőIsland Iced Tea Corp, for example, attracted investors in 2021 after it rebranded as Long Blockchain to take advantage ÔÇőof cryptocurrency mania. Its shares were later delisted, and the Securities and Exchange Commission brought insider trading charges , opens new tab .
In addition to raising $50 million ÔÇîin convertible Ôüáfinancing, Allbirds is playing the name game, too. It will now be known as NewBird AI, with a ÔÇťlong term vision to become a fully integrated GPU-as-a-Service and AI-native cloud solutions provider.ÔÇŁ The microscopic stock price jumped 620% on Wednesday.
ItÔÇÖs unclear what the company brings to the crowded and heavily capitalized world of data centers. CoreWeave (CRWV.O) , opens new tab , for one, is ÔÇőworth 400 times as much, ÔÇőhas close ties with ÔüáNvidia and just expanded chunky deals with Jane Street and Anthropic. Despite this progress, it is not clear what advantage it has over deep-pocketed and technologically advantaged rivals such ÔÇőas Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab and Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab .
In this context, NewBird AI looks like a considerable longshot. Its ÔÇőshoes may have Ôüáspread across Silicon Valley, but it will take more than a few dollars and a dream to get off the backfoot.
Follow Robert Cyran on Bluesky , opens new tab .

item.title: Exclusive: European buyers hold talks to ship Canadian LNG via Panama Canal to diversify supply
item.description:
CALGARY, April 15 (Reuters) - European buyers, including Germany's Uniper, are exploring the possibility of purchasing liquefied ÔÇőnatural gas from Canada's Pacific coast and shipping it through the Panama Canal as part of a long-term strategy to diversify supply, ÔÇîan effort made more urgent in the wake of the Iran war, two sources familiar with the matter said.
Three sources said European buyers are among the potential customers that have had commercial talks with Canada's Ksi Lisims LNG , a proposed liquefied natural gas export terminal whose backers have been working to finalize contracts with purchasers before making an expected final investment ÔÇődecision this year.
The interest in Ksi Lisims from potential European customers ÔÇö who include state-owned German energy group Uniper (UN0k.DE) , opens new tab , according to two sources ÔÇö ÔÇőis significant, as Canada's burgeoning LNG export industry is geographically more suited to supplying Asia and tolls and shipping time Ôüáthrough the Panama Canal will increase costs.
All of Canada's existing and emerging LNG export capacity is located on the country's west coast, offering short ÔÇőshipping times to Asian buyers, while its east coast has virtually no infrastructure beyond Repsol's < REP.MC , opens new tab > Saint John terminal.
Lack of infrastructure has long been seen as a ÔÇőbarrier to Canada supplying any meaningful amounts of LNG to Europe. But a source familiar with the Ksi Lisims LNG project said the current Middle East conflict means European buyers are considering accepting the costs and longer transit time of shipping through the Panama Canal in order to diversify their supply sources to a stable, democratic ÔÇőjurisdiction like Canada.
"Since the war in Iran started, there has been especially strong interest in (Ksi Lisims) offtake from LNG buyers all around the world, ÔÇőincluding from Europe," the source said.
Uniper declined to comment. Reuters reported in March that the German company was in talks with Canada at a corporate and political level to ÔÇîexpand its Ôüáliquefied natural gas purchases. The United States accounted for 96% of Germany's LNG imports last year, and two sources said Ksi Lisims is seen having potential when it comes to diversifying that supply.
The Ksi Lisims project is not an immediate solution to Europe's energy needs. Even if its proponents ÔÇö Houston-based Western LNG; a consortium of Canadian natural gas producers called Rockies LNG; and the Nisga'a First Nation, who own the land for the project ÔÇö decide ÔÇőto go ahead, it will be ÔÇőin construction for several years.
However, Ôüáthe project has been referred by the Canadian government to Canada's major projects office for fast-tracking. Prime Minister Mark Carney has sought to speed up natural resource project permitting to boost an economy that has been threatened by U.S. trade ÔÇőpolicy.
< SHEL.L , opens new tab > and < TTEF.PA , opens new tab > have already signed 20-year LNG purchase agreements with Ksi Lisims.
Canada's Energy and Natural Resources Minister said ÔÇőin August German companies are Ôüálooking to buy and swap Canadian LNG cargoes. But now, they are also interested in taking shipments of the physical Canadian product, the sources said.
The Iran war and the resulting Strait of Hormuz closure have made it increasingly likely that Ksi Lisims gets built, said Jamie Heard, vice president, capital markets for Tourmaline ÔüáOil (TOU.TO) , opens new tab , one ÔÇőof the Rockies LNG partners behind the project.
He said Western Canadian natural gas prices continue ÔÇőto lag behind the U.S. benchmark, making a strong business case for Canadian LNG.
"The economic rationale is there, these projects can pay themselves off quite quickly with spreads at that level," ÔÇőHeard said in an interview.

item.title: Bessent says U.S. won't be renewing waivers for Iranian and Russian oil
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The United States will not be renewing ÔÇőthe waivers that allowed purchase of some Iranian and ÔÇîRussian oil without facing U.S. sanctions, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told reporters on Wednesday.
Reuters reported on Tuesday that ÔÇőWashington would not renew a 30-day waiver of ÔÇősanctions on Iranian oil at sea that Ôüáexpires this week, and let a similar waiver ÔÇőon sanctions on Russian oil expire over the weekend.
"We ÔÇőwill not be renewing the general license on Russian oil, and we will not be renewing the general license ÔÇőon Iranian oil. That was oil that ÔÇőwas on the water prior to March 11. So all ÔÇîthat Ôüáhas been used," Bessent said at a White House briefing.
The moves signal an end to the Trump administration's efforts to use the sanctions waivers ÔÇőto free ÔÇőup more Ôüáoil supplies and lower soaring global energy prices.
The Iranian waiver, which the ÔÇőTreasury Department issued on March 20, allowed ÔÇősome Ôüá140 million barrels of oil to reach global markets and helped relieve pressure on energy supply Ôüáduring ÔÇőthe war, Bessent said last ÔÇőmonth. The waiver is set to expire on April 19.

item.title: Pakistan weighs spot LNG as Iran war hits supply, ramps up domestic oil and gas
item.description:
ISLAMABAD, April 15 (Reuters) - Pakistan is considering buying ÔÇîliquefied natural gas (LNG) on the spot market to offset supply disruptions caused by the Iran war but would favour government-to-government deals to avoid having to pay steep premiums, its petroleum minister said.
Qatar's force majeure has forced ÔÇőPakistan to make costly spot purchases or find alternative fuels ahead of summer ÔÇődemand.
Spot LNG cargoes have surged to $20 to $30 per mmBtu amid the Middle East Ôüáconflict, Ali Pervaiz Malik, the petroleum minister told Reuters on Wednesday.
Purchases would depend on whether ÔÇőprices are acceptable to the power sector, including under existing government-to-government arrangements with Azerbaijan's SOCAR.
Pakistan has ÔÇőalso been routing some crude supplies via Saudi Arabia's Red Sea port of Yanbu to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, with Malik saying insurance costs on that route were lower than routes crossing or near Hormuz.
Pakistan ÔÇőimports nearly all of its oil, much of it via the Strait of Hormuz, and ÔÇőremains exposed to supply shocks despite cutting its LNG reliance in recent years as gas is still needed ÔÇîto Ôüámeet the country's peak summer power demand.
It has begun commercial output from its highest-ever producing oil and gas well, as it shores up domestic supply.
"We have arrangements in place to meet domestic and industrial requirements," Malik said, adding that gas disruptions have not led to major curbs, ÔÇőwith eight of 10 ÔÇőfertiliser plants operating.
Officials are Ôüáalso considering the use of costlier fuels such as furnace oil to limit load shedding, although at the expense of higher tariffs. Malik ÔÇőwarned that prolonged shortages could threaten food security.
Pakistan's Baragzai X-01 well in ÔÇőnorthwestern Khyber ÔüáPakhtunkhwa is producing about 15,000 barrels of oil per day and 45 million cubic feet of gas, with output expected to rise further, its state-run operator Oil and Gas Development Company Ltd (OGDC) (OGDC.PSX) , opens new tab Ôüásaid.
The well ÔÇőcould reach up to 25,000 bpd and 60 mmcfd ÔÇőof gas, making it Pakistan's highest-producing well, and may contribute around 10% of crude output while cutting the country's import ÔÇőbill by about $329 million annually, OGDC said.

item.title: Tarique named among TIMEÔÇÖs 100 Most Influential People
White House offers no hint of Iran war cost as it seeks military funding surge
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - White House budget director Russell Vought said on Wednesday he could not estimate the cost of the Iran war, as he defended President ÔÇőDonald Trump's request for a massive $1.5 trillion annual military budget against bipartisan criticism from U.S. lawmakers who cited the Pentagon's historic lack ÔÇîof financial accountability.
"We're not ready to come to you with a request. We're still working on it. We're working through to figure out what's needed," Vought told a hearing of the House of Representatives Budget Committee. "I don't have a ballpark."
The cost of the war with Iran, which Trump began alongside Israel on February 28, has remained an open question on Capitol ÔÇőHill. An initial $200 billion request for additional funding for the war met with stiff opposition in Congress last month.
Vought appeared before the panel to ÔÇődiscuss Trump's proposed budget for fiscal year 2027, with its $500 billion increase in military spending and 10% reduction for non-defense programs.
The Ôüárequest is intended to reflect Republican priorities heading into the November midterm elections, in which Trump's Republicans hope to retain control over the House of Representatives ÔÇőand the Senate but face growing public concern about the cost of living, energy prices and the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran .
Democrats took issue with Vought's ÔÇőassertions that healthcare, education and low-income energy assistance programs were marred by fraud.
"I'm so glad you asked about fraud, because you are coming back to ask for a $1.5 trillion budget for the Department of Defense," Democratic Representative Pramila Jayapal of Washington state told the budget director. "The Department of Defense is the only federal agency that has never passed an audit ... But ÔÇőyou're not going after any of that."
Vought said the administration is pursuing "inefficiencies" at the Pentagon.
"I don't think you're doing enough," said Republican Representative Glenn Grothman, who ÔÇőcalled for a Pentagon audit to be completed before Congress votes on defense spending.
"There is so much arrogance in that agency," added Grothman, of Wisconsin. "They just say we don't have ÔÇîto do Ôüáit on audit. We're so damn important. We don't care what Congress thinks."
Vought promoted Trump's budget proposal for the fiscal year beginning October 1 as aimed at reducing spending. He promoted Trump's 2025 tax-cut-and-spending package known as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" as an initiative that achieved $2 trillion in mandatory savings through cuts to Medicaid health coverage and food assistance to low-income families.
That bill, which extended 2017 tax cuts, will add $4.7 trillion to U.S. deficits over the next decade, while reduced ÔÇőimmigration will add another $500 billion, according to ÔÇőthe nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office.
Representative Brendan Boyle of Pennsylvania, the budget panel's top Democrat, pointed to forecasts saying the legislation's healthcare cuts would mean the loss of health coverage for more than 15 million people. Vought said they were able-bodied adults, people in the country ÔÇőillegally or ineligible for benefits.
"You're going to sit here with a straight face and say they're all illegals? They ÔÇőwere all defrauding Ôüáthe system? That's actually your position?" Boyle asked.
"Yes," Vought replied.
Democratic Representative Scott Peters of California pointed out to Vought that the watchdog Government Accountability Office has found the administration illegally withheld billions of dollars allocated for National Institutes of Health grants, public schools and Head Start early education programs nationwide.
"Do you dispute GAO's findings?" Peters asked.
"Yes. GAO Ôüáis typically ÔÇőwrong. They're very partisan," Vought replied.
To become law, Trump's proposed budget needs approval from Congress ÔÇőat a time when Republicans are trying to overcome Democratic opposition to funding for Trump's immigration crackdown , just months after the longest government shutdown in U.S. history . Democrats have already declared the budget proposal dead ÔÇőon arrival, leaving government funding to closed-door negotiations between appropriators.

item.title: US envoy Waltz appears to torpedo Bachelet as next UN leader
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S. envoy to the United Nations, Mike Waltz, appeared to torpedo the ÔÇőcandidacy of former Chilean President Michelle Bachelet to be the next leader of the world body on ÔÇîWednesday by saying he shared a U.S. senator's concerns about her fitness for the job.
At a U.S. Senate committee hearing, Republican Senator Pete Ricketts of Nebraska charged that Bachelet had pulled punches as U.N. human rights chief in a 2022 report in failing to label China's ÔÇőactions against Uyghur Muslims as genocide, and has also promoted abortion as a fundamental human right.
Waltz said he ÔÇőwas not currently in a position to say whom the United States would support to Ôüáreplace U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres, and whom it would oppose. But he said in response to Ricketts, "I share your concerns."
He ÔÇőadded that he was sure that U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio also shared the concerns.
A new United Nations secretary-general will ÔÇőbe elected this year for a five-year term starting on January 1, 2027.
Support of the five permanent veto-wielding members of the U.N. Security Council - the United States, Russia, China, Britain and France - is vital for a candidate to succeed.
Bachelet also fell foul of China over the ÔÇőUyghur report, which said the detention of Uyghurs and other Muslims in the country's Xinjiang province may constitute crimes ÔÇőagainst humanity.
A representative for Bachelet's campaign did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Waltz said the conventional wisdom and in New York, where ÔÇîthe ÔüáUnited Nations has its headquarters, was that because there had never been a female secretary general, or one from Latin America, that the future U.N. leader should therefore be a Latin American woman.
"We have taken the position of we just need the best," he said. "And this institution desperately needs strong, effective leadership."
Waltz said, "reform, reform, reform ... and getting back to basics ÔÇőon peace and security will be ÔÇőat the top of Ôüáour criteria."
So far four candidates have been nominated, including Bachelet, Chile's first female head of state, who twice served as president of the South American nation.
In March, Chile withdrew its ÔÇőbacking for her candidacy after a change in leadership in the country and a ÔÇősharp shift to Ôüáthe right, but she said she would press ahead with support from Brazil and Mexico.
Bachelet was U.N. high commissioner for human rights from 2018-22 and executive director of U.N. Women from 2010-13.
The other declared candidates are Rafael Grossi, a veteran Argentine diplomat Ôüáwho is ÔÇőcurrently director-general of the International Atomic Energy Agency, Rebeca Grynspan, a former vice ÔÇőpresident of Costa Rica, and Macky Sall, the former president of Senegal.
The candidates are due to take part in interactive dialogues , opens new tab next week at the United ÔÇőNations that will be broadcast live, starting with Bachelet on Tuesday.

item.title: 'Significant' fire breaks out at Viva Energy's refinery in Australia
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Firefighters have ÔÇőarrived on the scene to respond to a ÔÇťsignificantÔÇŁ ÔÇîfire at Viva Energy Group's Geelong refinery in southern Australia, according to local fire authorities.
The extent of the damage was not immediately clear. ÔÇőThe blaze was reported at around 11 p.m. ÔÇőlocal time on Wednesday, Fire Rescue Victoria said in Ôüáa statement, adding it was not yet under control ÔÇőbut all staff had been accounted for.
The dispatch to the ÔÇőrefinery in Corio in the state of Victoria came after multiple calls reporting explosions and flames, it said.
The refinery can process up to ÔÇő120,000 barrels of oil per day, according to the company's ÔÇőwebsite.
It is one of two refineries remaining in Australia and employs over ÔÇî1,100 Ôüápeople, supplying over 50 percent of VictoriaÔÇÖs and 10 percent of AustraliaÔÇÖs fuel, the website said.
Viva Energy Group did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
Australia has faced ÔÇőfuel security ÔÇőconcerns since Iran Ôüáall but closed the Strait of Hormuz after being attacked by the U.S. and ÔÇőIsrael.
Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said last month ÔÇőthe Ôüágovernment would halve the excise on fuel and diesel and remove the heavy road user charge for three months to Ôüáhelp ÔÇőhouseholds cope with a surge in ÔÇőcosts driven by the Iran war.
