item.title: China added more crude to its massive stockpile in March, but outlook shifts
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, April 16 (Reuters) - China continued to build the world's largest stockpile of crude oil in March even as the rest of the world started to draw on inventories to compensate for the loss of millions of barrels from the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
China, ÔÇőthe world's biggest crude importer, had surplus oil of 1.74 million barrels per day (bpd) in March, according to calculations based on official data.
For the first quarter ÔÇîChina's excess crude was 1.41 million bpd, down from the record high of 2.67 million bpd in December but up from the average of 1.13 million bpd for the whole of 2025.
China does not disclose the volumes of crude flowing into or out of its strategic and commercial stockpiles, but an estimate can be made by deducting the amount of oil processed from the total crude available from imports and domestic output.
It ÔÇőis worth noting that not all the surplus crude was likely to have been added to storage, with some being processed in plants not captured by the official ÔÇődata.
But even allowing for those gaps, it is clear that from March 2025 onwards, China has been importing crude at a far higher rate Ôüáthan necessary to meet domestic fuel demand.
China's imports of crude oil were 11.77 million bpd in March, while domestic output was 4.49 million bpd.
Refinery processing was 14.52 million bpd, leaving a surplus ÔÇőof 1.74 million bpd available for storage.
The continuing building of China's crude stockpiles in March came as imports were largely unaffected by the conflict in the Middle East, which started on February ÔÇő28 when the United States and Israel launched an aerial campaign against Iran.
The oil delivered to China in March would have exited the Strait of Hormuz prior to the conflict's start, but imports from April onwards are likely to be affected.
This raises the question as to how China responds to lower crude imports from April onwards, which may be further constrained if the U.S. military is successful in executing President Donald Trump's ÔÇőorder to blockade vessels from Iranian ports.
China's seaborne crude imports in April are estimated by commodity analysts Kpler at 8.7 million bpd, and while this figure is likely to increase as ÔÇőmore cargoes are assessed as arriving prior to month end, it's still likely that arrivals will be the lowest since the 7.97 million bpd in August 2022.
China can tap some of its vast stockpile ÔÇîof crude oil Ôüáif it wants to keep domestic refinery processing around current rates.
The exact amount in both commercial and strategic inventories is not disclosed by Beijing, but analyst estimates are that it is at least 1.2 billion barrels.
If China did release up to 2 million bpd from reserves in order to maintain refinery throughput, it would take almost two years for the stockpile to deplete.
This is obviously an extreme situation and the reality is that crude oil flows from the Middle East will at some point return to levels close to what they were prior to the ÔÇőstart of the conflict.
This means China could easily ÔÇőmaintain refinery processing, and even increase it Ôüáto allow for exports of fuels, something Beijing has placed informal restrictions on since the start of the conflict.
China exported 1.19 million bpd of refined fuels in March, according to calculations based on official customs data, but this is likely to drop sharply in April, with Kpler ÔÇőtracking shipments of about 524,000 bpd for the month.
The loss of refined fuel exports from China has tightened an already stressed market ÔÇőfor products in Asia, with Ôüájet fuel and diesel both hitting record high prices during March.
It's likely that Beijing is expecting the Iran conflict to end in the coming weeks and that the flow of crude and products from the Middle East will resume.
However, if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for an extended period the risks mount for China.
While it can maintain domestic fuel supplies for an extended period because of Ôüáits huge crude ÔÇőreserves, if Asian neighbours experience shortages and their economies start to shut down it won't take long for ÔÇőthis to blow back on China's export-focused industries.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ÔÇőever. ROI can help you keep up. The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: Spain's Repsol wins back control of Venezuelan oil operations, FT says
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Spanish energy group Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab is ÔÇőpoised to take back operational control of its ÔÇîVenezuelan oil assets and boost production following a deal signed with the South American government, the Financial Times reported on ÔÇőThursday.
Repsol is expected to announce the agreement as ÔÇőearly as Thursday, FT added, citing a Ôüáperson familiar with the matter.
The agreement will include ÔÇőplans to triple production from its Venezuelan oil operations within ÔÇőthree years and establish a "guaranteed" payment system that will avoid previous pitfalls under which the capital city of Caracas failed ÔÇőto pay up, according to the report.
Reuters could ÔÇőnot immediately verify the report. Repsol did not immediately respond to ÔÇîReuters' Ôüárequest for a comment.
Venezuela holds one of the largest oil reserves in the world but has dilapidated energy infrastructure.
In 2023, Repsol reached an agreement with ÔÇőVenezuela to continue ÔÇőoperating its Ôüáfacilities there. The deal later lapsed after U.S. President Donald Trump revoked licences granted ÔÇőto Repsol and other Western companies to ÔÇőoperate Ôüáin the country.
After the U.S. captured President Nicolas Maduro in January, Washington eased sanctions on Venezuela's energy sector, Ôüáissuing general ÔÇőlicences that allow global energy ÔÇőcompanies to operate oil and gas projects in the OPEC member.

item.title: Thailand's PTT offers more fuel oil for May loading, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 16 (Reuters) - Thai state-owned energy company PTT has offered a ÔÇőlarger volume of high-sulphur ÔÇîfuel oil (HSFO) for loading in May than in previous months, according ÔÇőto market sources.
PTT offered four ÔÇőcargoes of HSFO in a Ôüátender that closes on ÔÇőThursday, sources said. The cargoes will ÔÇőload from the port of Sriracha.
One of the cargoes consist of 18,000 ÔÇőtons of fuel oil ÔÇőscheduled to load between May 5 and ÔÇî7, Ôüáthe sources said, while the other three cargoes each consist of 25,000 tons, scheduled to ÔÇőload ÔÇőbetween May Ôüá15-17, May 23-25, and May 29-31, respectively.
The ÔÇőrefiner typically offers one ÔÇőto Ôüátwo HSFO cargoes for loading per month via Sriracha, based Ôüáon ÔÇőpast records.
Companies typically do ÔÇőnot comment on commercial tenders.

item.title: Duke Energy seeks price increase in North Carolina to recoup winter power costs
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Duke Energy (DUK.N) , opens new tab has sought approval from North Carolina regulators to raise its prices and ÔÇőrecover more than $800 million from customers for ÔÇîhigher fuel and power purchase costs incurred during an extreme winter cold snap.
It was seeking to recover winter ÔÇőfuel and power costs totaling about $500 million ÔÇőat Duke Energy Carolinas and $309 million at ÔüáDuke Energy Progress, it said on Wednesday, ÔÇőwhich would on approval raise monthly bills by ÔÇőabout $6.90 and $7.88, respectively, starting June 1.
Duke Energy Carolinas serves about 2.3 million customers in central and western North Carolina, ÔÇőwhile Duke Energy Progress supplies about 1.6 ÔÇőmillion customers in the central and eastern parts of the ÔÇîstate.
The Ôüáextreme cold in late January and early February drove demand beyond the existing generation and storage capacity, forcing it to buy additional power from ÔÇőneighboring utilities ÔÇőat high Ôüámarket prices, the company said.
Duke Energy has proposed spreading the recovery over ÔÇő19 months, instead of the typical ÔÇő12-month Ôüáperiod, to limit the immediate impact on customers.
It said electricity demand hit a new winter high Ôüáof ÔÇő37,308 megawatt-hours on January 27, ÔÇőthe highest level ever recorded across Duke Energy's Carolinas system.
